abstract: A generalist robot should perform effectively across various environments. However, most existing approaches heavily rely on scaling action-annotated data to enhance their capabilities. Consequently, they are often limited to single physical specification and struggle to learn transferable knowledge across different embodiments and environments. To confront these limitations, we propose UniVLA, a new framework for learning cross-embodiment vision-language-action (VLA) policies. Our key innovation is to derive task-centric action representations from videos with a latent action model. This enables us to exploit extensive data across a wide spectrum of embodiments and perspectives. To mitigate the effect of task-irrelevant dynamics, we incorporate language instructions and establish a latent action model within the DINO feature space. Learned from internet-scale videos, the generalist policy can be deployed to various robots through efficient latent action decoding. We obtain state-of-the-art results across multiple manipulation and navigation benchmarks, as well as real-robot deployments. UniVLA achieves superior performance over OpenVLA with less than 1/20 of pretraining compute and 1/10 of downstream data. Continuous performance improvements are observed as heterogeneous data, even including human videos, are incorporated into the training pipeline. The results underscore UniVLA's potential to facilitate scalable and efficient robot policy learning.

keyInsights: Explicitly decoupling task-centric dynamics from task-irrelevant visual changes is crucial for learning robust and effective latent action representations from noisy, large-scale video datasets.
Utilizing semantically rich visual features like DINOv2 as reconstruction targets for latent action learning allows models to focus on meaningful task-related movements over pixel-level noise.

A unified, embodiment-agnostic latent action space enables significant knowledge transfer across diverse data sources, including human videos, fostering generalist robot policies with reduced computational overhead.
originalProblem: Robot policies heavily rely on expensive, ground-truth action labels, limiting scalability to new tasks, robots, or environments.
Heterogeneous action and observation spaces across different robot embodiments hinder knowledge transfer and the development of unified generalist policies.
Existing latent action learning methods often capture task-irrelevant dynamics (e.g., camera shakes, background motion), resulting in noisy and ineffective action representations.
results: Achieved 95.2% average success on the LIBERO manipulation benchmark (18.7% improvement over OpenVLA) and 47.1% oracle success on the Room2Room navigation benchmark (29.6% improvement over OpenVLA).
Demonstrated robust real-world robot deployment with an 81.7% average success rate and an average score of 2.63 on an AgileX Piper arm, outperforming LAPA by 36.7% in success rate.
Showcased superior data efficiency, achieving higher success rates on LIBERO-Goal (86.3%) with only 10% of demonstration data compared to OpenVLA trained on the full dataset (79.2%).
solution: A two-stage task-centric latent action learning framework that uses DINOv2 features and language conditioning to explicitly decouple task-relevant actions from environmental noise.
Pretraining a generalist policy on a Prismatic-7B Vision-Language Model (VLM), extending its vocabulary with special tokens for learned latent actions to leverage vast, unannotated video data.
A lightweight action decoder and Parameter-Efficient Fine-tuning (PEFT) for efficient deployment, incorporating historical latent actions into prompts to enhance sequential decision-making.
paper_summary.summary: A generalist robot policy framework learns task-centric latent actions from internet-scale, action-label free videos. This approach achieves average success rates of 95.2% on LIBERO and 47.1% on Room2Room, demonstrating efficient, cross-embodiment generalization and robust real-world performance.

title: UniVLA: Learning to Act Anywhere with Task-centric Latent Actions

abstract:
Imitation learning has emerged as a promising approach towards building
generalist robots. However, scaling imitation learning for large robot
foundation models remains challenging due to its reliance on high-quality
expert demonstrations. Meanwhile, large amounts of video data depicting a wide
range of environments and diverse behaviors are readily available. This data
provides a rich source of information about real-world dynamics and
agent-environment interactions. Leveraging this data directly for imitation
learning, however, has proven difficult due to the lack of action annotation.
In this work, we present Unified World Models (UWM), a framework that allows
for leveraging both video and action data for policy learning. Specifically, a
UWM integrates an action diffusion process and a video diffusion process within
a unified transformer architecture, where independent diffusion timesteps
govern each modality. By controlling each diffusion timestep, UWM can flexibly
represent a policy, a forward dynamics, an inverse dynamics, and a video
generator. Through simulated and real-world experiments, we show that: (1) UWM
enables effective pretraining on large-scale multitask robot datasets with both
dynamics and action predictions, resulting in more generalizable and robust
policies than imitation learning, (2) UWM naturally facilitates learning from
action-free video data through independent control of modality-specific
diffusion timesteps, further improving the performance of finetuned policies.
Our results suggest that UWM offers a promising step toward harnessing large,
heterogeneous datasets for scalable robot learning, and provides a simple
unification between the often disparate paradigms of imitation learning and
world modeling. Videos and code are available at
this https URL

keyInsights: Maintaining independent diffusion timesteps for actions and future observations enables a single model to flexibly infer policies, forward dynamics, inverse dynamics, and perform video prediction from the same trained model.

Co-training with action-free video data, by effectively masking out actions through diffusion timesteps, enhances model generalization and robustness to out-of-distribution conditions without diluting the action learning signal.

The use of 'register tokens' in the Diffusion Transformer architecture improves performance, hypothesized to facilitate information exchange between distinct modalities (actions and latent image patches).
originalProblem: Scaling imitation learning (IL) for generalist robots is bottlenecked by the high cost and scarcity of high-quality, action-annotated robot demonstration data.

World modeling approaches, while leveraging abundant action-free video data, struggle to effectively translate temporal dynamics knowledge into robust, generalizable robotic controllers for action generation.

The fields of imitation learning (action generation) and world modeling (observation prediction) have largely developed disparately, missing opportunities for joint learning and mutual benefit from shared features.
results: UWM achieved the highest success rates (up to 20% higher than baselines) on real-world manipulation tasks in the DROID dataset, showing strong performance in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.

Co-training UWM with action-free videos consistently improved its performance, boosting generalization and robustness (e.g., from 0.86 to 0.92 in Stack-Bowls in-distribution and 0.76 to 0.84 in out-of-distribution scenarios).

UWM accurately predicted future image observations, demonstrated effective inverse dynamics by generating actions that adhered more closely to reference trajectories, and scaled more effectively with large pretraining datasets than other methods.
solution: Introduces Unified World Models (UWM), a diffusion-based framework that integrates an action diffusion process and an image (video) diffusion process into a single Diffusion Transformer architecture.

Employs independent diffusion timesteps for actions and next observations, allowing for flexible inference modes (policy, forward dynamics, inverse dynamics, video prediction) by manipulating these timesteps at inference time.
Trains the model on multitask robotic datasets with action labels and co-trains on action-free video data by fixing the action timestep to a fully noised state, effectively masking missing actions.
paper_summary.summary: Unified World Models (UWM), developed by researchers at the University of Washington and Toyota Research Institute, presents a diffusion-based framework that unifies robot policy learning and world modeling within a single transformer, enabling it to learn from both action-labeled robotic data and action-free video datasets. The model demonstrates improved task success rates and robustness on real-world robot manipulation tasks, outperforming existing baselines.

title: Unified World Models: Coupling Video and Action Diffusion for  Pretraining on Large Robotic Datasets

abstract: Recent video generation models demonstrate remarkable ability to capture complex physical interactions and scene evolution over time. To leverage their spatiotemporal priors, robotics works have adapted video models for policy learning but introduce complexity by requiring multiple stages of post-training and new architectural components for action generation. In this work, we introduce Cosmos Policy, a simple approach for adapting a large pretrained video model (Cosmos-Predict2) into an effective robot policy through a single stage of post-training on the robot demonstration data collected on the target platform, with no architectural modifications. Cosmos Policy learns to directly generate robot actions encoded as latent frames within the video model's latent diffusion process, harnessing the model's pretrained priors and core learning algorithm to capture complex action distributions. Additionally, Cosmos Policy generates future state images and values (expected cumulative rewards), which are similarly encoded as latent frames, enabling test-time planning of action trajectories with higher likelihood of success. In our evaluations, Cosmos Policy achieves state-of-the-art performance on the LIBERO and RoboCasa simulation benchmarks (98.5% and 67.1% average success rates, respectively) and the highest average score in challenging real-world bimanual manipulation tasks, outperforming strong diffusion policies trained from scratch, video model-based policies, and state-of-the-art vision-language-action models fine-tuned on the same robot demonstrations. Furthermore, given policy rollout data, Cosmos Policy can learn from experience to refine its world model and value function and leverage model-based planning to achieve even higher success rates in challenging tasks. We release code, models, and training data at this https URL

keyInsights: Pretrained video generation models inherently learn strong spatiotemporal priors from dynamic internet videos, which are crucial for robust low-level visuomotor control in robotics.

A simple single-stage fine-tuning approach with latent frame injection is sufficient to adapt a powerful video foundation model into a multi-functional robot policy without the need for bespoke architectural modifications.

Unifying the policy, world model, and value function within a single, pretrained video model architecture creates a more coherent and effective system for both direct control and model-based planning.
originalProblem: Adapting large foundation models for robotics often requires complex multi-stage training pipelines or custom architectural modifications.
Existing vision-language-action (VLA) models, typically trained on static image-text data, often lack the dynamic spatiotemporal priors essential for robust robot control.
Traditional robot learning systems frequently employ separate, loosely coupled modules for policy, world modeling, and value estimation, which can increase system complexity.
results: Achieved new state-of-the-art average success rates: 98.5% on LIBERO, 67.1% on RoboCasa (with significantly fewer demonstrations), and 93.6% on real-world ALOHA bimanual tasks, outperforming leading baselines.

Ablation studies confirmed that the pretrained video model backbone improved performance by 3.9% on LIBERO and significantly more on real-world tasks, validating the importance of rich spatiotemporal priors.

Model-based planning, utilizing the learned world model and value function, yielded a 12.5-point average score increase on the most challenging ALOHA tasks, demonstrating effective guidance for decision-making even with limited rollout data.
solution: A large, pretrained latent video diffusion model (Cosmos-Predict2) is fine-tuned in a single stage, without architectural changes, to function as a unified robot policy, world model, and value function.

Diverse modalities such as robot proprioception, action chunks, state values, and multi-camera images are encoded as "latent frames" and injected directly into the video model's existing latent diffusion sequence.

A joint training scheme on robot demonstration and collected rollout data enables simultaneous learning of actions, future states, and expected rewards, leveraging auxiliary targets to enhance performance.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from NVIDIA and Stanford University fine-tune a large, pretrained latent video diffusion model, Cosmos-Predict2, into a unified robot policy for visuomotor control and planning. This method achieves state-of-the-art average success rates of 98.5% on LIBERO, 67.1% on RoboCasa, and 93.6% on ALOHA, demonstrating enhanced data efficiency and robustness for complex manipulation tasks.

title: Cosmos Policy: Fine-Tuning Video Models for Visuomotor Control and Planning

abstract: This work highlights that video world modeling, alongside vision-language pre-training, establishes a fresh and independent foundation for robot learning. Intuitively, video world models provide the ability to imagine the near future by understanding the causality between actions and visual dynamics. Inspired by this, we introduce LingBot-VA, an autoregressive diffusion framework that learns frame prediction and policy execution simultaneously. Our model features three carefully crafted designs: (1) a shared latent space, integrating vision and action tokens, driven by a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture, (2) a closed-loop rollout mechanism, allowing for ongoing acquisition of environmental feedback with ground-truth observations, (3) an asynchronous inference pipeline, parallelizing action prediction and motor execution to support efficient control. We evaluate our model on both simulation benchmarks and real-world scenarios, where it shows significant promise in long-horizon manipulation, data efficiency in post-training, and strong generalizability to novel configurations. The code and model are made publicly available to facilitate the community.

keyInsights: Decomposing robot control into visual dynamics prediction and action inference, using causal autoregressive modeling, allows for more efficient learning and explicit physical priors.
Incorporating persistent memory via KV-cache and strict causal attention directly addresses limitations in reactivity and temporal consistency for long-horizon tasks.
Asynchronous inference, specifically when grounded by a Forward Dynamics Model, enables efficient real-time closed-loop control by mitigating latency and trajectory drift.
originalProblem: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models suffer from "representation entanglement," where a single network struggles to learn diverse knowledge, limiting sample efficiency and generalization.
Existing robotic world models often exhibit a "reactivity gap" due to open-loop or chunk-based generation, lack persistent long-term memory, and violate causality with non-autoregressive attention.
Reactive policies in robotics often map observations directly to actions without explicitly modeling how the environment evolves, making them less robust to unforeseen changes.
results: LingBot-VA attained superior performance on challenging real-world tasks, achieving 97.0% Progress Score on "Make Breakfast" and robustly handling precision and deformable object manipulation.
The model established new state-of-the-art performance in simulation benchmarks, achieving 92.9% average success on RoboTwin 2.0 (Easy) and 98.5% average success on LIBERO.
It showed improved sample efficiency, requiring fewer demonstrations for adaptation, and stronger generalization capabilities to novel objects and out-of-distribution scenarios.
solution: Introduce LingBot-VA, an autoregressive diffusion framework that unifies video dynamics prediction and action inference by explicitly modeling the causal nature of the physical world.
Decompose robot control into two stages: predicting future visual observations and then inferring actions from current and predicted future states.
Employ a dual-stream diffusion transformer with causal attention and KV-cache to maintain long-term context and enable closed-loop, reactive control with real-time feedback.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed LingBot-VA, an autoregressive diffusion framework that unifies visual dynamics prediction and action inference for robot control. This model incorporates causal world modeling and persistent memory, enabling reactive closed-loop control and achieving state-of-the-art performance across diverse manipulation tasks in both simulation and real-world settings.

title: Causal World Modeling for Robot Control

abstract: Being able to simulate the outcomes of actions in varied environments will revolutionize the development of generalist agents at scale. However, modeling these world dynamics, especially for dexterous robotics tasks, poses significant challenges due to limited data coverage and scarce action labels. As an endeavor towards this end, we introduce DreamDojo, a foundation world model that learns diverse interactions and dexterous controls from 44k hours of egocentric human videos. Our data mixture represents the largest video dataset to date for world model pretraining, spanning a wide range of daily scenarios with diverse objects and skills. To address the scarcity of action labels, we introduce continuous latent actions as unified proxy actions, enhancing interaction knowledge transfer from unlabeled videos. After post-training on small-scale target robot data, DreamDojo demonstrates a strong understanding of physics and precise action controllability. We also devise a distillation pipeline that accelerates DreamDojo to a real-time speed of 10.81 FPS and further improves context consistency. Our work enables several important applications based on generative world models, including live teleoperation, policy evaluation, and model-based planning. Systematic evaluation on multiple challenging out-of-distribution (OOD) benchmarks verifies the significance of our method for simulating open-world, contact-rich tasks, paving the way for general-purpose robot world models.

keyInsights: Leveraging an unprecedented scale of egocentric human video data for pretraining allows world models to learn diverse physics and generalize to open-world robot tasks, overcoming robot data scarcity.

Continuous latent actions derived in a self-supervised manner can effectively serve as embodiment-agnostic proxy actions, enabling the transfer of causal interaction knowledge from unlabeled human videos to robot control.

Architectural enhancements like relative actions and chunked action injection, combined with a temporal consistency loss and distillation, are essential for achieving precise action controllability and real-time inference in robot world models.
originalProblem: Developing generalist robots is hindered by the difficulty of creating world models that can robustly simulate diverse, real-world action outcomes.
Existing robot world models are limited by expensive and scarce robot-specific data, high-dimensional continuous action spaces, and a lack of explicit action labels in readily available human videos.
Current video world models struggle to generalize to unseen objects, environments, or predict the consequences of counterfactual actions.
results: DreamDojo showed consistent improvements in simulation quality and generalization across novel evaluation sets by scaling human video data, outperforming baselines in human preference evaluations on out-of-distribution scenarios.
A distillation pipeline enabled the model to run at 10.81 FPS on a single H100 GPU, achieving a nearly 4x speedup with robust long-horizon consistency over 1-minute rollouts.

The model proved effective for downstream robot tasks, correlating with real-world success rates (Pearson r=0.995) for policy evaluation and improving model-based planning success by up to 17% (2x) for a fruit packing task.
solution: Curated DreamDojo-HV, a large-scale dataset of 44,711 hours of egocentric human videos, to provide diverse physical interaction data.
Introduced self-supervised continuous latent actions to extract causal information from unlabeled human videos, making them usable for action-conditioned world modeling.
Developed a foundation world model architecture incorporating relative actions, chunked action injection, and a temporal consistency loss, followed by a distillation pipeline for real-time inference.
paper_summary.summary: DreamDojo introduces a foundation world model that learns to simulate diverse, dexterous robot tasks by training on an unprecedented 44,711 hours of egocentric human video data. The model achieves real-time inference at 10.81 FPS and demonstrates a strong correlation (Pearson r=0.995) between simulated and real-world policy evaluation success rates.

title: DreamDojo: A Generalist Robot World Model from Large-Scale Human Videos

abstract: State-of-the-art Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models excel at semantic generalization but struggle to generalize to unseen physical motions in novel environments. We introduce DreamZero, a World Action Model (WAM) built upon a pretrained video diffusion backbone. Unlike VLAs, WAMs learn physical dynamics by predicting future world states and actions, using video as a dense representation of how the world evolves. By jointly modeling video and action, DreamZero learns diverse skills effectively from heterogeneous robot data without relying on repetitive demonstrations. This results in over 2x improvement in generalization to new tasks and environments compared to state-of-the-art VLAs in real robot experiments. Crucially, through model and system optimizations, we enable a 14B autoregressive video diffusion model to perform real-time closed-loop control at 7Hz. Finally, we demonstrate two forms of cross-embodiment transfer: video-only demonstrations from other robots or humans yield a relative improvement of over 42% on unseen task performance with just 10-20 minutes of data. More surprisingly, DreamZero enables few-shot embodiment adaptation, transferring to a new embodiment with only 30 minutes of play data while retaining zero-shot generalization.

keyInsights: Jointly predicting future world states (video) and actions with a video diffusion model provides powerful spatiotemporal priors, enabling zero-shot generalization to unseen physical motions and environments.
Generalist policies can be learned effectively from diverse, non-repetitive robot data, reducing the reliance on laborious, repetitive demonstrations.
Cross-embodiment transfer is highly efficient with WAMs, allowing learning from video-only demonstrations and few-shot adaptation to new robot platforms, crucial for scaling with human video data.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models struggle with generalizing to unseen physical motions in novel environments due to limited spatiotemporal priors from static image-text pretraining.
Learning generalist robot policies traditionally requires extensive, repetitive, task-specific demonstrations, which is inefficient and scales poorly to diverse, real-world scenarios.
Inefficient cross-embodiment transfer, often requiring new action-labeled data for each robot platform or human demonstration.
results: Achieved 62.2% average task progress on seen tasks in unseen environments, outperforming state-of-the-art pretrained VLAs (27.4%).
Demonstrated 39.5% average task progress on 10 entirely unseen tasks, markedly higher than VLA baselines (16.3%).
Enabled 42% relative improvement in unseen task performance for cross-embodiment transfer using only 10-20 minutes of video-only demonstrations from other robots or humans.
DreamZero-Flash achieved real-time control at 7Hz (150ms inference) with 74% task progress on table bussing, recovering most of the performance of 4-step DreamZero (83%).
solution: Introduces DreamZero, a 14B World Action Model (WAM) that uses an autoregressive diffusion transformer backbone to jointly predict future video frames and robot actions.
Leverages a pretrained video diffusion backbone and flow-matching objective to learn world physics and spatiotemporal dynamics from diverse robot data.
Implements DreamZero-Flash with asynchronous execution and optimizations like decoupled noise schedules to achieve real-time closed-loop control at 7Hz.
paper_summary.summary: DreamZero, a 14B World Action Model (WAM) developed by NVIDIA, enables zero-shot robot policies by jointly predicting future video states and actions from diverse data. It achieves an average task progress of 39.5% on unseen tasks and demonstrates 42% relative improvement in cross-embodiment transfer using video-only demonstrations, all while maintaining real-time control at 7Hz through significant optimization.

title: World Action Models are Zero-shot Policies

abstract:
The field of robotics has made significant strides toward developing
generalist robot manipulation policies. However, evaluating these policies in
real-world scenarios remains time-consuming and challenging, particularly as
the number of tasks scales and environmental conditions change. In this work,
we demonstrate that world models can serve as a scalable, reproducible, and
reliable proxy for real-world robot policy evaluation. A key challenge is
generating accurate policy videos from world models that faithfully reflect the
robot actions. We observe that directly inputting robot actions or using
high-dimensional encoding methods often fails to generate action-following
videos. To address this, we propose Policy2Vec, a simple yet effective approach
to turn a video generation model into a world simulator that follows latent
action to generate the robot video. We then introduce WorldEval, an automated
pipeline designed to evaluate real-world robot policies entirely online.
WorldEval effectively ranks various robot policies and individual checkpoints
within a single policy, and functions as a safety detector to prevent dangerous
actions by newly developed robot models. Through comprehensive paired
evaluations of manipulation policies in real-world environments, we demonstrate
a strong correlation between policy performance in WorldEval and real-world
scenarios. Furthermore, our method significantly outperforms popular methods
such as real-to-sim approach.

keyInsights: World model-based evaluation achieves stronger correlation with real-world performance than real-to-sim approaches
Policy2Vec action encoding outperforms traditional methods like VQVAE and one-hot encoding for video generation
Fréchet Inception Distance can serve as a lightweight metric for rapid policy ranking on simple tasks
Video generation models can effectively simulate complex manipulation scenarios when properly conditioned on policy actions
originalProblem: Real-world robot policy evaluation is time-consuming, expensive, and potentially dangerous for both equipment and operators
Traditional simulation-based evaluation suffers from the reality gap, failing to accurately predict real-world performance
Existing evaluation methods cannot scale to assess the growing complexity of modern robot manipulation policies
Need for automated safety detection to identify hazardous behaviors before real-world deployment
results: Achieves 0.79 Pearson correlation coefficient with real-world performance across multiple manipulation tasks
Outperforms real-to-sim evaluation methods in both MMRV (Mean Maximum Rank Violation) and correlation metrics
Successfully identifies unsafe policies that exhibit hazardous behaviors before real-world testing
Demonstrates cost-effective evaluation capabilities that can reduce reliance on expensive real-world testing protocols
solution: Develop WorldEval framework that uses world models to generate videos of policy execution for evaluation
Introduce Policy2Vec method that extracts latent action representations from policy networks and injects them into video generation models
Create automated evaluation pipeline using large language models (Gemini-2.0) to classify task success in generated videos
Implement safety detection capabilities to identify irregular or dangerous policy behaviors
paper_summary.summary: Midea Group and East China Normal University researchers develop WorldEval, a world model-based evaluation framework that assesses robot manipulation policies by generating videos of task execution using Policy2Vec action encoding and automated LLM-based success detection, achieving 0.79 Pearson correlation with real-world performance across multiple robotic tasks while outperforming real-to-sim evaluation methods and demonstrating capability to identify unsafe policies before deployment, with results showing that video generation approaches can provide more reliable policy assessment than traditional simulation-based methods for real-world robotics applications.

title: WorldEval: World Model as Real-World Robot Policies Evaluator

abstract:
We introduce $\textbf{F}$uture $\textbf{LA}$tent $\textbf{RE}$presentation
Alignment ($\textbf{FLARE}$), a novel framework that integrates predictive
latent world modeling into robot policy learning. By aligning features from a
diffusion transformer with latent embeddings of future observations,
$\textbf{FLARE}$ enables a diffusion transformer policy to anticipate latent
representations of future observations, allowing it to reason about long-term
consequences while generating actions. Remarkably lightweight, $\textbf{FLARE}$
requires only minimal architectural modifications -- adding a few tokens to
standard vision-language-action (VLA) models -- yet delivers substantial
performance gains. Across two challenging multitask simulation imitation
learning benchmarks spanning single-arm and humanoid tabletop manipulation,
$\textbf{FLARE}$ achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming prior
policy learning baselines by up to 26%. Moreover, $\textbf{FLARE}$ unlocks the
ability to co-train with human egocentric video demonstrations without action
labels, significantly boosting policy generalization to a novel object with
unseen geometry with as few as a single robot demonstration. Our results
establish $\textbf{FLARE}$ as a general and scalable approach for combining
implicit world modeling with high-frequency robotic control.

keyInsights: Implicit world modeling through latent alignment is more efficient than explicit visual prediction
Action-free human demonstration data can supplement robot learning to improve generalization
Exponential Moving Average helps stabilize training across distribution shifts
originalProblem: Traditional world modeling approaches that generate future visual frames face computational overhead and learning inefficiency
Robots struggle to generalize skills to novel objects and scenarios from limited demonstrations
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on multitask imitation learning benchmarks
95% success rate in real-world evaluations with just 100 trajectories per task
Demonstrated generalization to novel objects even with single robot demonstrations
Required minimal architectural modifications to existing Vision-Language-Action systems
solution: Future Latent Representation Alignment (FLARE) predicts compact future state representations rather than full visual frames
Action-aware observation embedding model combines with diffusion transformer policy for efficient learning
Co-training mechanism allows learning from human videos without action labels
paper_summary.summary: NVIDIA and university researchers introduce FLARE, a framework that integrates predictive world modeling into robot policy learning through latent space alignment, achieving up to 26% improvement over baselines while enabling robots to learn from human demonstration videos without action labels.

title: FLARE: Robot Learning with Implicit World Modeling

abstract: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) have garnered significant attention for their potential in advancing robotic manipulation. However, previous approaches predominantly rely on the general comprehension capabilities of vision-language models (VLMs) to generate action signals, often overlooking the rich temporal and causal structure embedded in visual observations. In this paper, we present UniVLA, a unified and native multimodal VLA model that autoregressively models vision, language, and action signals as discrete token sequences. This formulation enables flexible multimodal tasks learning, particularly from large-scale video data. By incorporating world modeling during post-training, UniVLA captures causal dynamics from videos, facilitating effective transfer to downstream policy learning--especially for long-horizon tasks. Our approach sets new state-of-the-art results across several widely used simulation benchmarks, including CALVIN, LIBERO, and Simplenv-Bridge, significantly surpassing previous methods. For example, UniVLA achieves 95.5% average success rate on LIBERO benchmark, surpassing pi0-FAST's 85.5%. We further demonstrate its broad applicability on real-world ALOHA manipulation and autonomous driving.

author_info.institution: Institute of Automation, Chinese Academy of Sciences

keyInsights: Unifying vision, language, and action into a single token-based, autoregressive framework enables tighter cross-modal integration and learning of temporal and causal dynamics.
Training a world model on abundant action-free video data significantly improves downstream policy learning efficiency and generalization, addressing robot data scarcity.
Visual prediction capabilities, even during fine-tuning, naturally integrate world model learning into policy learning, leading to performance improvements.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often rely on a language-centric paradigm and late-fusion strategies, limiting deep cross-modal integration.
Prior VLA approaches struggle to capture temporal and causal dependencies within the perception-action loop, particularly for complex, long-horizon tasks.
The scarcity and high cost of collecting action-annotated robotic demonstration data restrict the scalability and generalizability of policy learning.
results: UniVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance on the CALVIN benchmark and sets new SOTA across all LIBERO suites with an average success rate of 95.5%, improving LIBERO-Long to 94.0%.
World model post-training provides substantial performance gains, enabling higher success rates on CALVIN using only 10% of the fine-tuning data and faster convergence.

The model demonstrates multimodal capabilities beyond policy learning, including spatial reasoning via text output and future state prediction via visual output, with promising preliminary results in autonomous driving.
solution: UniVLA develops a unified multimodal model that represents vision, language, and action as discrete tokens within a shared vocabulary, processed by an 8.5 billion parameter autoregressive Transformer.

A two-stage training paradigm is employed: 1) post-training a world model on large-scale action-free video datasets to learn environment dynamics by predicting future visual content, and 2) fine-tuning for policy learning using action-annotated data.
The model frames embodied planning as sequence modeling, where observations and actions are causally interleaved, leveraging the autoregressive nature to capture temporal dependencies.
paper_summary.summary: UniVLA introduces a unified vision-language-action (VLA) model that processes all modalities as discrete tokens within a shared autoregressive Transformer. This framework achieves state-of-the-art results on robotic manipulation benchmarks like CALVIN, LIBERO (95.5% average success rate), and SimplerEnv by leveraging a world model trained on large-scale action-free video data.

title: Unified Vision-Language-Action Model

abstract:
A unified video and action model holds significant promise for robotics,
where videos provide rich scene information for action prediction, and actions
provide dynamics information for video prediction. However, effectively
combining video generation and action prediction remains challenging, and
current video generation-based methods struggle to match the performance of
direct policy learning in action accuracy and inference speed. To bridge this
gap, we introduce the Unified Video Action model (UVA), which jointly optimizes
video and action predictions to achieve both high accuracy and efficient action
inference. The key lies in learning a joint video-action latent representation
and decoupling video-action decoding. The joint latent representation bridges
the visual and action domains, effectively modeling the relationship between
video and action sequences. Meanwhile, the decoupled decoding, powered by two
lightweight diffusion heads, enables high-speed action inference by bypassing
video generation during inference. Such a unified framework further enables
versatile functionality through masked input training. By selectively masking
actions or videos, a single model can tackle diverse tasks beyond policy
learning, such as forward and inverse dynamics modeling and video generation.
Via an extensive set of experiments, we demonstrate that UVA can serve as a
general-purpose solution for a wide range of robotics tasks, such as policy
learning, forward/inverse dynamics and video observation prediction, without
compromising performance compared to methods tailored for specific
applications. Results are best viewed on
this https URL

keyInsights: Jointly modeling video and action within a unified latent space enables learning a more comprehensive understanding of scene dynamics, leading to improved action accuracy and robustness.
Decoupling video and action decoding via separate lightweight diffusion heads allows for fast action inference by bypassing computationally intensive video generation during policy execution.

A masked training framework provides a flexible, general-purpose architecture, enabling a single model to perform various robotic functionalities such as policy learning, video generation, and dynamics modeling.
originalProblem: Existing visuomotor policies either prioritize fast action prediction (action-only methods) or robust world understanding through video generation (video-first methods), leading to trade-offs in speed or accuracy and error propagation.
Developing robotic systems often requires multiple specialized models for different functionalities like policy execution, video generation, and dynamics modeling, increasing complexity.
Robot policies frequently struggle with generalization to visual disturbances, out-of-distribution scenarios, and multi-task settings, often failing to effectively leverage longer temporal contexts.
results: UVA achieved competitive or superior policy success rates on single-task benchmarks (e.g., 0.98 on PushT) and notably improved multi-task performance (e.g., 0.88 on PushT-M, 0.90 on Libero10, and up to 40% higher in real-world multi-task OOD scenarios) compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
Maintained inference speeds comparable to action-only policies (e.g., 0.23s vs 0.22s for Diffusion Policy in simulation), making it suitable for real-time robot control.

Demonstrated superior robustness to visual disturbances and effectively utilized longer history lengths, while also outperforming prior methods in video generation quality (lower FVD) and reducing error for inverse dynamics modeling (0.75 cm position error vs. 1.92 cm).
solution: UVA learns a unified latent representation that jointly encodes historical visual observations and action sequences using a Transformer architecture.
It employs decoupled video and action diffusion heads that decode future video observations and action chunks independently, allowing for fast action inference by bypassing video generation.

A masked training framework is used to achieve versatile functionality, enabling a single model to act as a robot policy, video generator, forward dynamics model, or inverse dynamics model by selectively masking inputs and outputs.
paper_summary.summary: The Unified Video Action Model (UVA) from Stanford University introduces a general-purpose robotic learning framework that jointly models video and action to achieve high action accuracy and efficient inference. UVA demonstrates competitive or superior performance across multiple simulation and real-world robot tasks, including multi-task scenarios and under visual disturbances, while maintaining fast inference speeds for real-time operation.

title: Unified Video Action Model

abstract: Generalization in robot manipulation is essential for deploying robots in open-world environments and advancing toward artificial general intelligence. While recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models leverage large pre-trained understanding models for perception and instruction following, their ability to generalize to novel tasks, objects, and settings remains limited. In this work, we present VideoVLA, a simple approach that explores the potential of transforming large video generation models into robotic VLA manipulators. Given a language instruction and an image, VideoVLA predicts an action sequence as well as the future visual outcomes. Built on a multi-modal Diffusion Transformer, VideoVLA jointly models video, language, and action modalities, using pre-trained video generative models for joint visual and action forecasting. Our experiments show that high-quality imagined futures correlate with reliable action predictions and task success, highlighting the importance of visual imagination in manipulation. VideoVLA demonstrates strong generalization, including imitating other embodiments' skills and handling novel objects. This dual-prediction strategy - forecasting both actions and their visual consequences - explores a paradigm shift in robot learning and unlocks generalization capabilities in manipulation systems.

keyInsights: A strong positive correlation exists between the quality of the robot's predicted visual imagination and the reliability of its corresponding action predictions, suggesting imagination as an implicit indicator of action accuracy.

Leveraging the physical plausibility and generalization capabilities embedded in large video generation models, trained on massive real-world videos, can effectively enhance robotic planning and execution.
Jointly modeling video (future visual outcomes) and action modalities within a single generative framework leads to a powerful and consistent perception-action loop for generalizable robot control.
originalProblem: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, often built on understanding-oriented foundation models, exhibit limited true generalization to unseen tasks, novel objects, or entirely unfamiliar environments.
Deploying robots in diverse, unpredictable open-world environments requires significantly more robust generalization than existing systems can provide.
The dominant paradigm in VLA research has not fully exploited the inherent physical understanding and predictive capabilities present in large-scale generative models.
results: VideoVLA achieved the highest average performance across 12 tasks in simulation (SIMPLER environment) and real-world pick, stack, and place tasks, outperforming state-of-the-art VLA baselines.

The model demonstrated superior generalization to novel objects, achieving a 65.2% average success rate on 10 unseen objects in simulation and successfully handling all 12 novel objects in real-world pick-and-place tasks.

It exhibited robust cross-embodiment skill transfer, outperforming the second-best model by 28.2 percentage points on average for 8 novel skills in simulation and achieving the highest success rates across all 6 evaluated novel skills in real-world scenarios.
solution: VideoVLA transforms a large pre-trained video generation model (CogVideoX) into a generalizable robotic manipulator by adapting its Diffusion Transformer (DiT) backbone.
The system is designed to jointly predict both a sequence of robot actions and a corresponding video clip depicting the anticipated future visual outcomes.

It uses a unified multi-modal input sequence, concatenating language instruction tokens, the current visual observation's latent, and targets (future frame latents and action chunk) for joint denoising within a DDPM framework.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Microsoft Research Asia, Xi'an Jiaotong University, and Fudan University developed VideoVLA, a robot manipulator that repurposes large pre-trained video generation models. This system jointly predicts future video states and corresponding actions, achieving enhanced generalization capabilities for novel objects and skills in both simulated and real-world environments.

title: VideoVLA: Video Generators Can Be Generalizable Robot Manipulators

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for robot learning, but their representations are still largely inherited from static image-text pretraining, leaving physical dynamics to be learned from comparatively limited action data. Generative video models, by contrast, encode rich spatiotemporal structure and implicit physics, making them a compelling foundation for robotic manipulation. But their potentials are not fully explored in the literature. To bridge the gap, we introduce DiT4DiT, an end-to-end Video-Action Model that couples a video Diffusion Transformer with an action Diffusion Transformer in a unified cascaded framework. Instead of relying on reconstructed future frames, DiT4DiT extracts intermediate denoising features from the video generation process and uses them as temporally grounded conditions for action prediction. We further propose a dual flow-matching objective with decoupled timesteps and noise scales for video prediction, hidden-state extraction, and action inference, enabling coherent joint training of both modules. Across simulation and real-world benchmarks, DiT4DiT achieves state-of-the-art results, reaching average success rates of 98.6% on LIBERO and 50.8% on RoboCasa GR1 while using substantially less training data. On the Unitree G1 robot, it also delivers superior real-world performance and strong zero-shot generalization. Importantly, DiT4DiT improves sample efficiency by over 10x and speeds up convergence by up to 7x, demonstrating that video generation can serve as an effective scaling proxy for robot policy learning. We release code and models at this https URL.

keyInsights: Video generation, by inherently capturing motion priors and causal structures, serves as a more effective and sample-efficient proxy objective for robotic policy learning compared to semantic-centric baselines.
Extracting features from specific intermediate denoising steps of a video generative backbone provides stable and generalized action priors, balancing semantic content with pixel-level details.
Jointly optimizing video dynamics and action prediction through decoupled timesteps for different modalities allows for synergistic learning of visual understanding and inverse dynamics.
originalProblem: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, based on static image-text data, struggle to learn complex spatiotemporal dynamics and physical interactions from limited robotic data.
Existing generative video models, while capable of synthesizing future frames, have not been fully integrated as end-to-end foundational backbones for direct robotic policy learning.
Learning low-level physical interactions and temporal state transitions in robotics is data-intensive and computationally expensive, limiting scalability and sample efficiency.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art average success rates of 98.6% on the LIBERO benchmark and 50.8% on the RoboCasa-GR1 benchmark, surpassing VLM-based and parameter-matched baselines.
Demonstrated over 10x higher sample efficiency and up to 7x faster convergence for policy learning in simulation compared to semantic-centric baselines.

Exhibited robust zero-shot generalization to unseen objects, categories, and quantity variations in both simulation and real-world tasks on a Unitree G1 robot, outperforming other methods that collapsed in such scenarios.
solution: DiT4DiT presents a unified cascaded framework that couples a video Diffusion Transformer (DiT) with an action Diffusion Transformer.
It employs a dual flow-matching mechanism with an asymmetric tri-timestep scheme for video generation, feature extraction, and action prediction.

Intermediate hidden activations from the video DiT's denoising process are repurposed as rich visual tokens to condition the action DiT, providing stable inputs without full video generation during control.
paper_summary.summary: DiT4DiT introduces an end-to-end Video-Action Model that couples a video Diffusion Transformer with an action Diffusion Transformer, leveraging intermediate denoising features from video generation to condition action prediction. The method achieved state-of-the-art success rates of 98.6% on LIBERO and 50.8% on RoboCasa-GR1, demonstrating over 10x higher sample efficiency and superior zero-shot generalization compared to VLM-based baselines.

title: DiT4DiT: Jointly Modeling Video Dynamics and Actions for Generalizable Robot Control

abstract: World-Action Models (WAM) initialized from pre-trained video generation backbones have demonstrated remarkable potential for robot policy learning. However, existing approaches face two critical bottlenecks that hinder performance and deployment. First, jointly reasoning over future visual dynamics and corresponding actions incurs substantial inference overhead. Second, joint modeling often entangles visual and motion representations, making motion prediction accuracy heavily dependent on the quality of future video forecasts. To address these issues, we introduce GigaWorld-Policy, an action-centered WAM that learns 2D pixel-action dynamics while enabling efficient action decoding, with optional video generation. Specifically, we formulate policy training into two coupled components: the model predicts future action sequences conditioned on the current observation, and simultaneously generates future videos conditioned on the predicted actions and the same observation. The policy is supervised by both action prediction and video generation, providing richer learning signals and encouraging physically plausible actions through visual-dynamics constraints. With a causal design that prevents future-video tokens from influencing action tokens, explicit future-video generation is optional at inference time, allowing faster action prediction during deployment. To support this paradigm, we curate a diverse, large-scale robot dataset to pre-train an action-centered video generation model, which is then adapted as the backbone for robot policy learning. Experimental results on real-world robotic platforms show that GigaWorld-Policy runs 9x faster than the leading WAM baseline, Motus, while improving task success rates by 7%. Moreover, compared with pi-0.5, GigaWorld-Policy improves performance by 95% on RoboTwin 2.0.

keyInsights: Dense future visual dynamics, even when sparsely predicted, provide strong regularization and supervision for learning robust policies without needing explicit future video generation during inference.
A comprehensive pre-training strategy across diverse video datasets (web, embodied, robot-centric) effectively imbues the model with generalizable dynamics and embodiment-specific priors.
Causally structuring visual and action tokens allows for efficient, real-time control while preserving the benefits of world-model-based supervision for action plausibility and learning efficiency.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models suffer from sparse and low-diversity action supervision, leading to actions inferred from shallow signals rather than physical grounding.
Current World-Action Models (WAMs) incur high inference latency and error propagation due to mandatory, iterative future video generation during deployment.
results: Achieved an approximate 9x speedup in inference latency (360 ms) compared to the state-of-the-art WAM, Motus (3231 ms), on an NVIDIA A100 GPU.
Demonstrated an average real-world success rate of 0.83 across four manipulation tasks, surpassing Motus (0.76) by 7% and the VLA baseline "pi_0.5" (0.69) by 14%.
Exhibited improved data efficiency, matching the performance of the ".5" VLA baseline using only 10% of the training data.
solution: Proposes an action-centered World–Action model utilizing a 5B-parameter diffusion Transformer for joint action and sparse visual dynamics prediction.
Implements a multi-stage curriculum training from web videos to embodied data, then to target-robot trajectories, to adapt and specialize the model.
Uses a blockwise causal attention mask that decouples action generation from future video prediction at inference, enabling low-latency action-only operation.
paper_summary.summary: GigaWorld-Policy introduces an action-centered World–Action model that leverages dense visual dynamics for robust robotic policy learning while enabling efficient, low-latency action generation. It achieves a 9x speedup in inference over previous World–Action models and attains an 83% real-world success rate, outperforming prior methods.

title: GigaWorld-Policy: An Efficient Action-Centered World--Action Model

abstract: Learning transferable knowledge from unlabeled video data and applying it in new environments is a fundamental capability of intelligent agents. This work presents VideoWorld 2, which extends VideoWorld and offers the first investigation into learning transferable knowledge directly from raw real-world videos. At its core, VideoWorld 2 introduces a dynamic-enhanced Latent Dynamics Model (dLDM) that decouples action dynamics from visual appearance: a pretrained video diffusion model handles visual appearance modeling, enabling the dLDM to learn latent codes that focus on compact and meaningful task-related dynamics. These latent codes are then modeled autoregressively to learn task policies and support long-horizon reasoning. We evaluate VideoWorld 2 on challenging real-world handcraft making tasks, where prior video generation and latent-dynamics models struggle to operate reliably. Remarkably, VideoWorld 2 achieves up to 70% improvement in task success rate and produces coherent long execution videos. In robotics, we show that VideoWorld 2 can acquire effective manipulation knowledge from the Open-X dataset, which substantially improves task performance on CALVIN. This study reveals the potential of learning transferable world knowledge directly from raw videos, with all code, data, and models to be open-sourced for further research.

keyInsights: Explicitly disentangling action dynamics from visual appearance modeling is crucial for learning transferable knowledge from diverse, real-world video data.
Pretrained Video Diffusion Models can effectively handle visual appearance, allowing latent dynamic representations to focus purely on task-relevant actions.
Utilizing low-fidelity motion cues to condition the VDM, in a gradient-stopped manner, stabilizes training and enhances long-horizon sequence generation by refining appearance.
originalProblem: AI models primarily learning from text lack the rich information about world dynamics, spatial relationships, and physical laws present in visual data.
Current video generation models and latent action models struggle to extract transferable knowledge for complex, long-horizon real-world tasks, often overfitting to irrelevant visual details.
The entanglement of action dynamics and visual appearance in previous models hinders generalization and leads to degraded performance in novel environments.
results: Achieved a 68.8% to 72.3% success rate on step 7 of paper-folding tasks on the Video-CraftBench benchmark, representing up to a ~70% improvement over competing methods.
Demonstrated superior cross-domain transferability in CALVIN robotic manipulation, achieving an average task success length of 2.88 when pre-trained on OpenX, surpassing other baselines.
Produced high-quality, long-horizon task execution videos with improved visual fidelity, reflected in SSIM of 0.770 and LPIPS of 0.205, while effectively filtering out irrelevant visual details.
solution: A dynamics-enhanced Latent Dynamics Model (dLDM) is proposed to robustly acquire knowledge by explicitly decoupling appearance modeling from action learning.
A pretrained Video Diffusion Model (VDM) is leveraged for appearance generation, enabling dLDM's latent codes to focus on task-relevant dynamics without encoding fine-grained visual details.
An autoregressive transformer is then trained to predict sequences of these compact, disentangled latent codes, facilitating long-horizon planning and task execution.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from ByteDance Seed and Beijing Jiaotong University developed a dynamics-enhanced Latent Dynamics Model (dLDM) that effectively decouples action dynamics from visual appearance to learn transferable knowledge directly from unlabeled real-world videos. This method demonstrated superior success rates, achieving up to 72.3% on complex paper-folding tasks and significantly improving cross-domain task success on robotic manipulation benchmarks, while also generating high-quality, long-horizon task execution videos.

title: VideoWorld 2: Learning Transferable Knowledge from Real-world Videos

abstract: We introduce PhysWorld, a framework that enables robot learning from video generation through physical world modeling. Recent video generation models can synthesize photorealistic visual demonstrations from language commands and images, offering a powerful yet underexplored source of training signals for robotics. However, directly retargeting pixel motions from generated videos to robots neglects physics, often resulting in inaccurate manipulations. PhysWorld addresses this limitation by coupling video generation with physical world reconstruction. Given a single image and a task command, our method generates task-conditioned videos and reconstructs the underlying physical world from the videos, and the generated video motions are grounded into physically accurate actions through object-centric residual reinforcement learning with the physical world model. This synergy transforms implicit visual guidance into physically executable robotic trajectories, eliminating the need for real robot data collection and enabling zero-shot generalizable robotic manipulation. Experiments on diverse real-world tasks demonstrate that PhysWorld substantially improves manipulation accuracy compared to previous approaches. Visit \href{this https URL}{the project webpage} for details.

keyInsights: While video generation offers vast visual demonstrations, explicit physical world modeling is essential to bridge the gap between visual plausibility and physical feasibility for robotic tasks.
High-quality 3D geometry and physical properties can be effectively reconstructed from monocular, generated videos by integrating 4D reconstruction with generative priors.
Focusing on object motions as learning targets and using residual reinforcement learning within a physical simulation environment leads to more robust and efficient policy acquisition.
originalProblem: Generated videos, while visually plausible, lack physical accuracy, making direct translation to robot actions often infeasible or inaccurate in the real world.
Existing robot learning methods from generated videos either still require substantial real-world robot demonstrations or suffer from physically ungrounded, pixel-level imitation.
The high cost and labor intensity of collecting large-scale real-world robot data limit the scalability and generalizability of robotic manipulation systems.
results: Achieved an 82% average success rate across 10 diverse real-world manipulation tasks, outperforming baseline zero-shot visual imitation methods by a substantial margin (e.g., 15 percentage points over RIGVid).
The explicit physical world model reduced grasping failures from 18% to 3% and completely eliminated tracking failures observed in purely visual approaches.

Object-centric learning demonstrated superior robustness (up to 90% success) compared to embodiment-centric learning, with residual reinforcement learning accelerating policy convergence significantly.
solution: Introduces PhysWorld, a framework that generates task-conditioned videos and then reconstructs a physically interactable "digital twin" of the scene from the visual outputs.
Employs geometry-aligned 4D reconstruction, textured mesh generation with generative priors, and physical property estimation from the generated monocular videos.
Leverages an object-centric residual reinforcement learning approach to learn robust manipulation policies within the reconstructed physical world model.
paper_summary.summary: A new framework, PhysWorld, enables robots to learn and execute complex manipulation tasks in a zero-shot manner by generating physically feasible actions from task-conditioned videos. It rebuilds the physical world from generated visual data, leading to an 82% average success rate in real-world tasks and significantly reducing grasping failures from 18% to 3%.

title: Robot Learning from a Physical World Model

abstract: Video generation models are rapidly improving in their ability to synthesize human actions in novel contexts, holding the potential to serve as high-level planners for contextual robot control. To realize this potential, a key research question remains open: how can a humanoid execute the human actions from generated videos in a zero-shot manner? This challenge arises because generated videos are often noisy and exhibit morphological distortions that make direct imitation difficult compared to real video. To address this, we introduce a two-stage pipeline. First, we lift video pixels into a 4D human representation and then retarget to the humanoid morphology. Second, we propose GenMimic-a physics-aware reinforcement learning policy conditioned on 3D keypoints, and trained with symmetry regularization and keypoint-weighted tracking rewards. As a result, GenMimic can mimic human actions from noisy, generated videos. We curate GenMimicBench, a synthetic human-motion dataset generated using two video generation models across a spectrum of actions and contexts, establishing a benchmark for assessing zero-shot generalization and policy robustness. Extensive experiments demonstrate improvements over strong baselines in simulation and confirm coherent, physically stable motion tracking on a Unitree G1 humanoid robot without fine-tuning. This work offers a promising path to realizing the potential of video generation models as high-level policies for robot control.

keyInsights: Video generative models can serve as scalable high-level action planners for robots, provided the robot control policy is specifically designed to handle their inherent noise and morphological inaccuracies.
Using 3D keypoints as the goal observation space and applying weighted tracking rewards significantly improves a robot's ability to robustly mimic motions from noisy, generated sources.

Incorporating an inductive bias like bilateral symmetry loss into the policy training objective further enhances robustness, allowing the robot to perform more reliably even when presented with inconsistent or physically implausible motions.
originalProblem: Humanoid robots struggle to generalize to novel tasks specified by abstract, high-level commands without extensive task-specific training.
Video generative models produce outputs with inherent noise, morphological inconsistencies, and physical implausibilities, making them unsuitable for direct robot control.
Current humanoid control approaches often rely on expensive, high-quality human motion capture data, limiting scalability and adaptability to diverse, unforeseen scenarios.
results: GenMimic's teacher policy achieved an 86.77% success rate in simulation on the GenMimicBench dataset, significantly outperforming baselines (e.g., GMT 4.29%, TWIST 2.69%) on generated video inputs.
The policy successfully deployed on a 23-DoF Unitree G1 humanoid robot, reproducing a wide range of upper-body motions (e.g., waving, pointing) with a 1.0 visual success rate for in-place actions.

Ablation studies confirmed that both weighted keypoint rewards (increasing SR from 40.0% to 77.4%) and the symmetry loss (further increasing SR to 86.8%) were crucial for GenMimic's superior robustness and performance.
solution: GenMimic utilizes a two-stage pipeline: first, a 4D human reconstruction model extracts body poses and shapes from generated RGB videos, which are then retargeted to the robot's morphology as 3D keypoint trajectories.
A robust, physics-aware reinforcement learning policy is trained in simulation using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to mimic these retargeted 4D humanoid trajectories.

The policy incorporates weighted tracking rewards, prioritizing critical keypoints, and an auxiliary bilateral symmetry loss to enhance robustness against noisy, imperfect motion cues from generated videos.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from UC Berkeley and NYU developed GenMimic, a two-stage framework that enables humanoid robots to mimic human actions from synthetically generated, noisy videos in a zero-shot manner. The system achieved an 86.77% success rate in simulation on a new benchmark and successfully reproduced coherent upper-body motions on a physical Unitree G1 robot.

title: From Generated Human Videos to Physically Plausible Robot Trajectories

abstract: Human videos offer a scalable way to train robot manipulation policies, but lack the action labels needed by standard imitation learning algorithms. Existing cross-embodiment approaches try to map human motion to robot actions, but often fail when the embodiments differ significantly. We propose X-Sim, a real-to-sim-to-real framework that uses object motion as a dense and transferable signal for learning robot policies. X-Sim starts by reconstructing a photorealistic simulation from an RGBD human video and tracking object trajectories to define object-centric rewards. These rewards are used to train a reinforcement learning (RL) policy in simulation. The learned policy is then distilled into an image-conditioned diffusion policy using synthetic rollouts rendered with varied viewpoints and lighting. To transfer to the real world, X-Sim introduces an online domain adaptation technique that aligns real and simulated observations during deployment. Importantly, X-Sim does not require any robot teleoperation data. We evaluate it across 5 manipulation tasks in 2 environments and show that it: (1) improves task progress by 30% on average over hand-tracking and sim-to-real baselines, (2) matches behavior cloning with 10x less data collection time, and (3) generalizes to new camera viewpoints and test-time changes. Code and videos are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Object motion, rather than direct human actions, serves as a transferable and dense supervisory signal, effectively bridging the cross-embodiment gap for robot policy learning.
A photorealistic simulation reconstructed directly from human videos can be effectively leveraged as an intermediary to train robot policies without requiring any robot teleoperation data.

Online domain adaptation, which self-calibrates observation encoders by aligning real and simulated image embeddings, offers a robust method for sim-to-real transfer without needing real robot action labels or precise object tracking during deployment.
originalProblem: Robot learning, particularly for foundation models, faces a data scarcity problem due to the high cost and labor intensity of collecting robot-specific teleoperated demonstrations.
Transferring skills from abundant human videos to robots is challenging because of significant kinematic, dynamic, and visual differences between human and robot embodiments.
Policies trained exclusively in simulation often struggle with robust deployment in the real world due to the visual and physical discrepancies known as the sim-to-real gap.
results: X-SIM significantly outperformed hand-tracking baselines, achieving over 30% average gains in task progress in challenging cross-embodiment settings.
The framework demonstrated strong sim-to-real transfer capabilities with RGB image observations, further enhanced by an average 8% increase in task progress using online domain adaptation.

X-SIM showed superior data efficiency, successfully completing tasks with only 1 minute of human video data, compared to 10 minutes of robot teleoperation data required for comparable performance via behavior cloning.
solution: Transforms human RGBD videos into a photorealistic simulated environment by tracking 6D object poses with FoundationPose and reconstructing the scene using 2D Gaussian Splatting.

Trains a privileged-state Reinforcement Learning (RL) policy in simulation using object-centric rewards derived from human object trajectories, then generates a large dataset of synthetic image-action pairs.

Deploys an image-conditioned policy (Diffusion Policy) trained on synthetic data, enhanced by an online domain adaptation (Auto-Calibration) technique that aligns real and simulated image embeddings using a contrastive InfoNCE loss.
paper_summary.summary: A real-to-sim-to-real framework, X-SIM, enables training robot manipulation policies directly from human videos, achieving effective sim-to-real transfer through object-centric supervision and an online domain adaptation method, thereby removing the requirement for robot teleoperation data.

title: X-Sim: Cross-Embodiment Learning via Real-to-Sim-to-Real

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a popular paradigm for learning robot manipulation policies that can follow language instructions and generalize to novel scenarios. Recent works have begun to explore the incorporation of latent actions, abstract representations of motion between two frames, into VLA pre-training. In this paper, we introduce villa-X, a novel Vision-Language-Latent-Action (ViLLA) framework that advances latent action modeling for learning generalizable robot manipulation policies. Our approach improves both how latent actions are learned and how they are incorporated into VLA pre-training. We demonstrate that villa-X can generate latent action plans in a zero-shot fashion, even for unseen embodiments and open-vocabulary symbolic understanding. This capability enables villa-X to achieve superior performance across diverse simulation tasks in SIMPLER and on two real-world robotic setups involving both gripper and dexterous hand manipulation. These results establish villa-X as a principled and scalable paradigm for learning generalizable robot manipulation policies. We believe it provides a strong foundation for future research.

keyInsights: Physically grounding latent actions with proprioceptive feedback significantly improves their quality and utility for low-level robot control, bridging abstract visual changes with concrete physical dynamics.

A joint diffusion framework, which explicitly factors the policy into latent and robot action experts, provides a more structured and potent mechanism for transferring planning knowledge to robot execution.
Leveraging diverse, action-free human videos through physically grounded latent actions enables robust zero-shot generalization to unseen robot embodiments and open-vocabulary instructions.
originalProblem: The scarcity of large-scale, high-quality robot data with corresponding action labels limits the training of robust Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models.
Existing latent action models, primarily based on visual signals, often produce physically ungrounded representations, hindering effective knowledge transfer to real robot control.
Current methods for integrating latent actions into VLA pre-training lack structured and effective mechanisms for transferring information to low-level robot policies.
results: `villa-X` achieved state-of-the-art performance on the SIMPLER benchmark, with an average success rate of 77.7% for the Google robot and 62.5% for the WidowX robot, surpassing prior VLA and latent-action methods.

The framework demonstrated strong zero-shot generalization, successfully planning and executing tasks on unseen Realman and XArm robots (the latter without dexterous-hand pretraining) with novel objects and open-vocabulary commands.

Ablation studies confirmed that the proprioceptive FDM enhanced latent action quality, leading to improved downstream policy performance, and the embodiment context vector improved reconstruction losses and generalization.
solution: A Latent Action Model (LAM) is developed with a proprioceptive Forward Dynamics Model (proprio-FDM) as an auxiliary decoder, compelling latent tokens to emphasize physical dynamics alongside visual changes.
An embodiment context vector is incorporated into the LAM's proprio-FDM to disambiguate heterogeneous robot morphologies and control frequencies present in large datasets.

An ACTor Module (ACT) employs a joint diffusion framework, explicitly modeling both latent and robot actions, where robot actions are conditioned on predicted latent plans, observation, and language features.
paper_summary.summary: Microsoft Research and collaborating universities introduce `villa-X`, a framework that enhances Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by developing physically grounded latent actions and integrating them via a joint diffusion policy. This approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across various simulation benchmarks and real-world robot manipulation tasks, exhibiting strong zero-shot generalization capabilities.

title: villa-X: Enhancing Latent Action Modeling in Vision-Language-Action Models

abstract:
Learning robot control policies from human videos is a promising direction
for scaling up robot learning. However, how to extract action knowledge (or
action representations) from videos for policy learning remains a key
challenge. Existing action representations such as video frames, pixelflow, and
pointcloud flow have inherent limitations such as modeling complexity or loss
of information. In this paper, we propose to use object-centric 3D motion field
to represent actions for robot learning from human videos, and present a novel
framework for extracting this representation from videos for zero-shot control.
We introduce two novel components in its implementation. First, a novel
training pipeline for training a ''denoising'' 3D motion field estimator to
extract fine object 3D motions from human videos with noisy depth robustly.
Second, a dense object-centric 3D motion field prediction architecture that
favors both cross-embodiment transfer and policy generalization to background.
We evaluate the system in real world setups. Experiments show that our method
reduces 3D motion estimation error by over 50% compared to the latest method,
achieve 55% average success rate in diverse tasks where prior approaches
fail~($\lesssim 10$\%), and can even acquire fine-grained manipulation skills
like insertion.

keyInsights: Object-centric representation focusing on desired object motion rather than human hand movements enables embodiment-agnostic transfer while preserving essential 3D geometric information
Simulation-trained denoising estimators can effectively handle real-world sensor noise when trained with comprehensive noise augmentation that mimics actual depth sensor characteristics
Dense camera intrinsic information (coordinate maps and inverse focal lengths) is critical for accurate 3D motion field reconstruction, as shown by significant performance drops when removed
Diffusion models combined with masking augmentation are essential for learning fine-grained manipulation policies that can handle precise tasks like insertion from human video demonstrations
originalProblem: Robot learning is severely bottlenecked by expensive and time-consuming robot-specific data collection, limiting scalability and generalization

Existing action representations for learning from human videos suffer from critical limitations: raw video frames are noisy and redundant, 2D pixel flow loses essential 3D information, point cloud 3D flow is too noisy, and SE(3) poses require pre-existing 3D object models
Real-world depth sensors produce inherently noisy data with missing values and boundary errors, making direct 3D motion computation inaccurate
Cross-embodiment transfer from human demonstrations to robots remains challenging due to morphological differences and domain gaps
results: Achieved over 50% reduction in 3D motion estimation error compared to direct baseline methods when tested on real robot manipulation videos with known ground truth transformations

Demonstrated ~55% average zero-shot success rate across five diverse real-world manipulation tasks (pick-rotate-place, line tracking, tool pushing, wrench tightening, precise insertion) while prior approaches achieved ≲10% success rates
Successfully performed precise insertion task with 2.5mm tolerance solely from human video training, representing unprecedented fine-grained manipulation capability in this setting
Showed strong robustness to injected Gaussian noise in depth observations and background variations during deployment, validating the denoising capabilities and object-centric design
solution: Introduce object-centric 3D motion field as a superior action representation that preserves dense 3D information while being embodiment-agnostic and compatible with image-based generative models

Develop a two-phase framework: Phase I trains a denoising 3D motion field estimator on large-scale synthetic data (8M samples) with realistic noise augmentation, Phase II uses this estimator to label human videos for robot policy training

Design dual-head UNet architecture with dense intrinsic map features containing camera coordinate and focal length information to enable accurate 3D motion reconstruction from 2D pixel movements and noisy depth
Employ diffusion models for policy learning with object masking data augmentation to bridge the visual domain gap between human hands and robot grippers
