paper_summary.summary: BAIR researchers at UC Berkeley develop an object-centric 3D motion field framework that enables robots to learn manipulation tasks directly from human demonstration videos by extracting dense 3D movement information for each observable object point, achieving over 50% reduction in 3D motion estimation error compared to direct methods through a novel denoising estimator trained on 8 million synthetic samples, and demonstrating ~55% zero-shot success rates across five real-world manipulation tasks including precise insertion with 2.5mm tolerance, while prior video-based approaches achieved ≲10% success rates, accomplished through a two-phase pipeline that first trains a robust motion field estimator on noisy simulated RGBD data then uses it to label human videos for training embodiment-agnostic robot policies with diffusion models and object masking augmentation to bridge the domain gap between human hands and robot grippers.

title: Object-centric 3D Motion Field for Robot Learning from Human Videos

abstract: Action-conditioned video models offer a promising path to building general-purpose robot simulators that can improve directly from data. Yet, despite training on large-scale robot datasets, current state-of-the-art video models still struggle to predict physically consistent robot-object interactions that are crucial in robotic manipulation. To close this gap, we present PlayWorld, a simple, scalable, and fully autonomous pipeline for training high-fidelity video world simulators from interaction experience. In contrast to prior approaches that rely on success-biased human demonstrations, PlayWorld is the first system capable of learning entirely from unsupervised robot self-play, enabling naturally scalable data collection while capturing complex, long-tailed physical interactions essential for modeling realistic object dynamics. Experiments across diverse manipulation tasks show that PlayWorld generates high-quality, physically consistent predictions for contact-rich interactions that are not captured by world models trained on human-collected data. We further demonstrate the versatility of PlayWorld in enabling fine-grained failure prediction and policy evaluation, with up to 40% improvements over human-collected data. Finally, we demonstrate how PlayWorld enables reinforcement learning in the world model, improving policy performance by 65% in success rates when deployed in the real world.
title: PlayWorld: Learning Robot World Models from Autonomous Play

abstract: World models, which predict future transitions from past observation and action sequences, have shown great promise for improving data efficiency in sequential decision-making. However, existing world models often require extensive domain-specific training and still produce low-fidelity, coarse predictions, limiting their usefulness in complex environments. In contrast, video diffusion models trained on large-scale internet data have demonstrated impressive capabilities in generating high-quality videos that capture diverse real-world dynamics. In this work, we present Vid2World, a general approach for leveraging and transferring pre-trained video diffusion models into interactive world models. To bridge the gap, Vid2World systematically explores video diffusion causalization, reshaping both the architecture and training objective of pre-trained models to enable autoregressive generation. Additionally, it incorporates a causal action guidance mechanism to enhance action controllability in the resulting interactive world models. Extensive experiments across multiple domains, including robot manipulation, 3D game simulation, and open-world navigation, demonstrate that our method offers a scalable and effective pathway for repurposing highly capable video diffusion models into interactive world models.
title: Vid2World: Crafting Video Diffusion Models to Interactive World Models

abstract: World Models (WMs) have emerged as a promising approach for post-training Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies to improve robustness and generalization under environmental changes. However, most WM-based post-training methods rely on pixel-space supervision, making policies sensitive to pixel-level artifacts and hallucination from imperfect WM rollouts. We introduce World2Act, a post-training framework that aligns VLA actions directly with WM video-dynamics latents using a contrastive matching objective, reducing dependence on pixels. Post-training performance is tied to rollout quality, yet current WMs struggle with arbitrary-length video generation as they are mostly trained on fixed-length clips while robotic execution durations vary widely. To address this, we propose an automatic LLM-based skill-decomposition pipeline that segments high-level instructions into low-level prompts. Our pipeline produces RoboCasa-Skill and LIBERO-Skill, supporting skill-compositional WMs that remain temporally consistent across diverse task horizons. Empirically, applying World2Act to VLAs like GR00T-N1.6 and Cosmos Policy achieves state-of-the-art results on RoboCasa and LIBERO, and improves real-world performance by 6.7%, enhancing embodied agent generalization.

keyInsights: Aligning VLA actions directly with World Model (WM) video-dynamics latents via contrastive learning reduces sensitivity to pixel-level noise and artifacts inherent in WM rollouts.
Decomposing complex robotic tasks into atomic skills using an LLM enables WMs to generate temporally consistent and stable arbitrary-length videos, overcoming limitations of fixed-length training.
Employing a lightweight residual policy for post-training is more computationally efficient than full fine-tuning, allowing faster integration of WM dynamic priors into VLA backbones.
originalProblem: Embodied AI policies trained with behavior cloning lack robust dynamics priors, limiting generalization to varied or novel environmental conditions.
Existing World Model-based post-training methods rely on pixel-space supervision, making them sensitive to artifacts and hallucinations in WM rollouts, especially over long task horizons.
World Models trained on fixed-length video clips struggle to generate temporally consistent, arbitrary-length videos for diverse robotic tasks, leading to error accumulation.
results: World2Act achieved state-of-the-art success rates on RoboCasa (66.3% for Cosmos Policy with fewer demonstrations) and improved GR00T-N1.6-ft performance by 2.5% (72.6%) compared to DreamGen.

Skill-compositional World Models (WMs) consistently enhanced success rates over Base-WMs (e.g., 1.1% gain on RoboCasa) and demonstrated faster, more stable convergence in video-action latent alignment.

The method showed improved performance with increased post-training trajectories and strong cross-task generalization, yielding a 6.67% average success rate increase in real-world robot experiments with minimal inference overhead.
solution: A latent action post-training framework, World2Act, directly aligns VLA actions with World Model (WM) video-dynamics latents using a contrastive matching objective, bypassing pixel-space supervision.
An automatic LLM-based pipeline decomposes high-level task instructions into atomic skill prompts, creating new skill-compositional datasets (RoboCasa-Skill, LIBERO-Skill) for stable WM training.
A skill-compositional WM leverages these decomposed skills to generate temporally consistent, arbitrary-length video rollouts by chaining sub-videos corresponding to each atomic skill.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at MBZUAI developed World2Act, a latent action post-training framework that transfers dynamic priors from skill-compositional World Models to Vision-Language-Action policies. The method reduces reliance on noisy pixel-space supervision, achieving improved success rates on complex manipulation tasks in simulation (e.g., 66.3% on RoboCasa) and real-world environments (6.67% average gain) while maintaining high inference speed.

title: World2Act: Latent Action Post-Training via Skill-Compositional World Models

abstract: Scaling action-controllable world models is limited by the scarcity of action labels. While latent action learning promises to extract control interfaces from unlabeled video, learned latents often fail to transfer across contexts: they entangle scene-specific cues and lack a shared coordinate system. This occurs because standard objectives operate only within each clip, providing no mechanism to align action semantics across contexts. Our key insight is that although actions are unobserved, their semantic effects are observable and can serve as a shared reference. We introduce Seq$\Delta$-REPA, a sequence-level control-effect alignment objective that anchors integrated latent action to temporal feature differences from a frozen, self-supervised video encoder. Building on this, we present Olaf-World, a pipeline that pretrains action-conditioned video world models from large-scale passive video. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method learns a more structured latent action space, leading to stronger zero-shot action transfer and more data-efficient adaptation to new control interfaces than state-of-the-art baselines.

keyInsights: Explicitly aligning latent actions with observable temporal differences (effects) in semantic features from a frozen self-supervised encoder is crucial for achieving context-invariance and preventing shortcut learning.
The cross-context non-identifiability problem in latent action learning can be overcome by using a global reference based on control-effect alignment.
Pretraining world models with such transferable latent actions significantly improves data efficiency for adaptation and generalization to novel, unseen visual contexts.
originalProblem: Acquiring large-scale, frame-aligned action labels for action-controllable world models is expensive and lacks generalization.
Existing latent action learning methods suffer from shortcut learning, capturing scene-specific visual cues instead of robust control signals.
Learned latent action spaces often lack a shared coordinate system across different visual contexts, leading to cross-context non-identifiability.
results: Achieved higher Macro-F1 scores in cross-context linear probing compared to baselines, demonstrating that Seq∆-REPA learns context-invariant and linearly decodable latent actions.
Demonstrated superior zero-shot action transfer, reliably reproducing intended motions in new scenes/viewpoints while preserving target scene appearance and subject consistency.

Obtained the lowest Relative Pose Error (RPE) across all adaptation budgets (0, 1, and 50 labeled videos), showing highly data-efficient adaptation and improved generalization to out-of-distribution scenes.
solution: Develops Seq∆-REPA (Sequence-level Control-Effect Alignment), a novel objective that aligns integrated latent actions with observable semantic effect directions derived from a frozen self-supervised video encoder.
Utilizes a pretrained large-scale latent image-to-video diffusion transformer backbone and injects the learned context-invariant latent actions as conditioning.
Proposes a data-efficient adaptation mechanism using a lightweight action adapter and LoRA fine-tuning for specific interactive environments with minimal labeled data.
paper_summary.summary: Olaf-World introduces a framework for pretraining action-conditioned video world models from unlabeled video data. It achieves context-invariant and transferable latent actions by aligning them with observable semantic changes, leading to robust zero-shot action transfer and data-efficient adaptation to new control interfaces.

title: Olaf-World: Orienting Latent Actions for Video World Modeling

abstract: Vision-based robotic policies often struggle with even minor viewpoint changes, underscoring the need for view-invariant visual representations. This challenge becomes more pronounced in real-world settings, where viewpoint variability is unavoidable and can significantly disrupt policy performance. Existing methods typically learn invariance from multi-view observations at the scene level, but such approaches rely on visual appearance and fail to incorporate the physical dynamics essential for robust generalization. We propose View-Invariant Latent Action (VILA), which models a latent action capturing transition patterns across trajectories to learn view-invariant representations grounded in physical dynamics. VILA aligns these latent actions across viewpoints using an action-guided objective based on ground-truth action sequences. Experiments in both simulation and the real world show that VILA-based policies generalize effectively to unseen viewpoints and transfer well to new tasks, establishing VILA as a strong pretraining framework that improves robustness and downstream learning performance.

keyInsights: Enforcing viewpoint invariance on the *dynamics* (latent actions) rather than the entire static visual scene leads to more principled and effective representations for robust visuomotor control.

Utilizing both local (Weighted InfoNCE) and global (structural loss) similarity constraints, guided by ground-truth actions, is crucial for learning a well-structured and view-invariant latent action space.
Learning dynamics-aware, view-invariant representations during pre-training provides a strong, data-efficient prior for adapting policies to new tasks and generalizing to unseen viewpoints.
originalProblem: Vision-based robotic policies are brittle to changes in camera viewpoint, severely degrading performance in real-world deployment scenarios.
Existing methods often enforce viewpoint invariance at the static scene level, which is an overly demanding task and conflates static context with dynamic, task-relevant information.
Collecting extensive multi-view calibrated datasets is costly and challenging to scale, while current data augmentation techniques have limitations in achieving robust generalization.
results: VILA achieved the best unseen-view success rates across all five simulated tasks and two real-world tasks, notably maintaining 93.1% success on Lift for extrapolated views where baselines largely failed.

The framework provided a consistently stronger and more data-efficient prior for unseen task adaptation, outperforming other multi-view and ImageNet pre-trained encoders, especially with limited demonstration data.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical contributions of both the action-guided Weighted InfoNCE and structural loss, with VILA's representations exhibiting the highest view entropy and lowest action entropy, indicating strong view invariance and dynamics-aware semantics.
solution: VILA proposes a two-stage pre-training framework that learns compact, view-invariant latent actions by explicitly grounding invariance in the physical dynamics of robot-environment interactions.

The first stage trains an Inverse Dynamics Model (IDM) and Forward Dynamics Model (FDM) with novel action-guided Weighted InfoNCE and structural losses to align latent action similarities with ground-truth action similarities across different viewpoints.

The second stage trains a latent behavior cloning policy to predict these view-invariant latent actions from current observations, which then serves as a robust vision encoder for downstream visuomotor tasks without requiring future frames.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Seoul National University developed VILA, a pre-training framework for vision-based robotic policies that achieves robustness to camera viewpoint changes by learning view-invariant latent actions grounded in physical dynamics. This approach significantly increased success rates in unseen views for both simulated and real-world robot manipulation tasks and enabled more data-efficient adaptation to new tasks.

title: Learning to Act Robustly with View-Invariant Latent Actions

abstract: Video action models (VAMs) have emerged as a promising paradigm for robot learning, owing to their powerful visual foresight for complex manipulation tasks. However, current VAMs, typically relying on either slow multi-step video generation or noisy one-step feature extraction, cannot simultaneously guarantee real-time inference and high-fidelity foresight. To address this limitation, we propose S-VAM, a shortcut video-action model that foresees coherent geometric and semantic representations via a single forward pass. Serving as a stable blueprint, these foreseen representations significantly simplify the action prediction. To enable this efficient shortcut, we introduce a novel self-distillation strategy that condenses structured generative priors of multi-step denoising into one-step inference. Specifically, vision foundation model (VFM) representations extracted from the diffusion model's own multi-step generated videos provide teacher targets. Lightweight decouplers, as students, learn to directly map noisy one-step features to these targets. Extensive experiments in simulation and the real world demonstrate that our S-VAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods, enabling efficient and precise manipulation in complex environments. Our project page is this https URL

keyInsights: The dilemma between real-time inference and high-fidelity visual foresight in VAMs can be resolved by directly distilling structured generative priors into single-step inference.

Supervising foresight decouplers with Vision Foundation Model (VFM) representations extracted from the diffusion model's *own* generated videos is essential to maintain trajectory alignment and effective distillation.
Dense patch-level semantic (e.g., DINOv2) and dynamic geometry-oriented (e.g., DPAv3) VFM targets are more effective for precise robotic manipulation than global or static alternatives.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often lack inherent spatiotemporal foresight, requiring extensive robot action data to learn physical dynamics.
Current Video-Action Models (VAMs) face a dilemma: high-fidelity visual foresight from multi-step video generation is too slow for real-time control.
Faster one-step feature extraction in VAMs yields noisy, entangled features that lack crucial temporal coherence, explicit geometry, and semantic distinctiveness for precise manipulation.
results: S-VAM achieved state-of-the-art performance on simulated benchmarks, with an average sequence length of 4.16 on CALVIN and a 72.8% average success rate on MetaWorld.
It outperformed existing methods in real-world multi-task manipulation, demonstrating a 32% success rate on the challenging "Place-to-Pot (Hard)" task compared to VPP's 16%.
The model maintains an effective real-time control frequency of 25 Hz, suitable for robotic applications, with a minor 15.8% latency overhead compared to VPP.
solution: Propose S-VAM, a novel framework that foresees coherent geometric and semantic representations in a single forward pass, bypassing multi-step video generation.

Implement a self-distillation strategy where geometric and semantic decoupler networks are supervised by VFM representations (DPAv3, DINOv2) extracted from the video diffusion model's *own* multi-step generated videos.
Integrate the distilled foresight with original diffusion features and task instructions into an action expert (Uni-Perceiver and Diffusion Policy) for real-time control.
paper_summary.summary: A shortcut video-action model, S-VAM, integrates real-time inference with high-fidelity geometric and semantic visual foresight by self-distilling structured generative priors from multi-step video diffusion. This approach demonstrably improves robotic manipulation performance in complex simulated and real-world tasks, for instance, achieving a 32% success rate on a difficult real-world task where prior methods reached 16%.

title: S-VAM: Shortcut Video-Action Model by Self-Distilling Geometric and Semantic Foresight

abstract: Action-conditioned robot world models generate future video frames of the manipulated scene given a robot action sequence, offering a promising alternative for simulating tasks that are difficult to model with traditional physics engines. However, these models are optimized for short-term prediction and break down when deployed autoregressively: each predicted clip feeds back as context for the next, causing errors to compound and visual quality to rapidly degrade. We address this through the following contributions. First, we introduce a reinforcement learning (RL) post-training scheme that trains the world model on its own autoregressive rollouts rather than on ground-truth histories. We achieve this by adapting a recent contrastive RL objective for diffusion models to our setting and show that its convergence guarantees carry over exactly. Second, we design a training protocol that generates and compares multiple candidate variable-length futures from the same rollout state, reinforcing higher-fidelity predictions over lower-fidelity ones. Third, we develop efficient, multi-view visual fidelity rewards that combine complementary perceptual metrics across camera views and are aggregated at the clip level for dense, low-variance training signal. Fourth, we show that our approach establishes a new state-of-the-art for rollout fidelity on the DROID dataset, outperforming the strongest baseline on all metrics (e.g., LPIPS reduced by 14% on external cameras, SSIM improved by 9.1% on the wrist camera), winning 98% of paired comparisons, and achieving an 80% preference rate in a blind human study.

keyInsights: Explicitly training world models on their own autoregressive outputs, rather than just ground-truth histories, is crucial to address the closed-loop gap and prevent error compounding.
Contrastive RL objectives can be effectively adapted for x0-prediction diffusion models to optimize for long-horizon generation stability, with theoretical convergence guarantees.
Combining diverse perceptual metrics across multiple camera views provides a robust and comprehensive reward signal for improving visual fidelity in complex, dynamic robotic scenes.
originalProblem: Action-conditioned video diffusion world models suffer from "exposure bias," where errors accumulate during long autoregressive rollouts.
This error accumulation leads to rapid visual degradation and loss of scene coherence, limiting their use as reliable robot simulators.
Current models are typically optimized for short-term, single-step prediction based on ground-truth history (teacher forcing), creating a mismatch with multi-step deployment.
results: The post-trained model achieved up to a 4.09 dB PSNR gain and a 46.6% LPIPS reduction on external camera views compared to baselines on the DROID dataset, with improvements concentrated in object- and robot-centric regions.

Human preference studies favored rollouts from the proposed model 80% of the time over the baseline in blind side-by-side comparisons, indicating qualitatively superior perceived realism and temporal consistency.

The world model demonstrated improved utility for policy evaluation, achieving a Pearson Correlation of 0.822 (vs. 0.796 baseline) and reduced MMRV (0.006 vs. 0.053 baseline) in simulated task progression.
solution: An online reinforcement learning (RL) post-training scheme is adapted, utilizing reward-contrasted denoising for x0-prediction diffusion models to improve stability.
A novel autoregressive training protocol generates multiple candidate futures from a shared, evolving rollout state, using a variable-length prefix to expose the model to various error regimes.
Dense, multi-view visual fidelity rewards are designed by combining LPIPS, SSIM, and PSNR across three camera views, aggregated at the clip level and group-normalized.
paper_summary.summary: A post-training reinforcement learning framework stabilizes action-conditioned video diffusion robot world models, enabling coherent multi-step rollouts over longer horizons (up to 11 seconds) by mitigating exposure bias. The method, developed at the Czech Institute of Informatics, Robotics and Cybernetics, achieves up to a 4.09 dB PSNR gain and 46.6% LPIPS reduction on external cameras on the DROID dataset and is preferred by humans in 80% of blind comparisons.

title: Persistent Robot World Models: Stabilizing Multi-Step Rollouts via Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Video action models are an appealing foundation for Vision--Language--Action systems because they can learn visual dynamics from large-scale video data and transfer this knowledge to downstream robot control. Yet current diffusion-based video predictors are trained with likelihood-surrogate objectives, which encourage globally plausible predictions without explicitly optimizing the precision-critical visual dynamics needed for manipulation. This objective mismatch often leads to subtle errors in object pose, spatial relations, and contact timing that can be amplified by downstream policies. We propose VAMPO, a post-training framework that directly improves visual dynamics in video action models through policy optimization. Our key idea is to formulate multi-step denoising as a sequential decision process and optimize the denoising policy with rewards defined over expert visual dynamics in latent space. To make this optimization practical, we introduce an Euler Hybrid sampler that injects stochasticity only at the first denoising step, enabling tractable low-variance policy-gradient estimation while preserving the coherence of the remaining denoising trajectory. We further combine this design with GRPO and a verifiable non-adversarial reward. Across diverse simulated and real-world manipulation tasks, VAMPO improves task-relevant visual dynamics, leading to better downstream action generation and stronger generalization. The homepage is this https URL.

keyInsights: Directly optimizing for control-relevant visual dynamics in diffusion models via RL significantly enhances their utility for robotic control, surpassing the limitations of purely likelihood-based training.

The selective application of stochasticity (Euler Hybrid sampler) at the most critical early denoising step provides stable and efficient policy gradient updates, mitigating long-horizon credit assignment.

Improvements in visual dynamics lead to a richer vision-action coupling, allowing downstream action models to leverage a larger number of independent visual directions for more sophisticated and nuanced control.
originalProblem: Diffusion-based video action models, trained with likelihood-surrogate objectives, do not explicitly optimize for the high-precision visual dynamics crucial for robotic control.
This objective mismatch leads to subtle errors in predicted object pose, spatial relationships, and contact timing, which are then amplified when consumed by downstream robotic policies.
Amplified errors result in compounding failures, reduced task success rates, and limit the robustness of VLA systems in fine-grained manipulation.
results: VAMPO achieved the highest task completion rates across various task lengths (1-10 steps) and the best average trajectory length on CALVIN ABC→D and L-CALVIN benchmarks, outperforming existing state-of-the-art VLM- and VPM-based methods.

The method demonstrated consistent improvements in video generation quality and reduced L1 loss in latent space across multiple real-world robot manipulation scenarios, including grasping and precise placement.

Ablation studies confirmed that GRPO was more effective than DDPO, and applying SDE-style denoising only at the first step yielded superior performance compared to multi-step stochasticity or pixel-level rewards.
solution: The multi-step denoising process of the Video Prediction Model (VPM) is re-framed as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) to enable direct policy optimization.
An Euler Hybrid sampler is introduced, applying SDE-style stochasticity only at the first denoising step, which enables tractable policy gradient estimation while preserving temporal consistency.

The VPM is fine-tuned using Group-normalized Proximal Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a verifiable relative latent-consistency reward that directly measures alignment with expert future visual dynamics.
paper_summary.summary: VAMPO introduces a post-training framework that optimizes the visual dynamics of diffusion-based video action models using reinforcement learning. This approach refines the precision of predicted visual dynamics, leading to improved action generation and robust performance in complex robotic manipulation tasks across simulated and real-world environments.

title: VAMPO: Policy Optimization for Improving Visual Dynamics in Video Action Models

abstract: Learning transferable latent actions from large-scale object manipulation videos can significantly enhance generalization in downstream robotics tasks, as such representations are agnostic to different robot embodiments. Existing approaches primarily rely on visual reconstruction objectives while neglecting physical priors, leading to sub-optimal performance in learning universal representations. To address these challenges, we propose a Universal Latent Action Learning framework that takes task instructions and multiple frames as inputs, and optimizes both future frame reconstruction and action sequence prediction. Unlike prior works, incorporating action predictions (e.g., gripper or hand trajectories and orientations) allows the model to capture richer physical priors such as real-world distances and orientations, thereby enabling seamless transferability to downstream tasks. We further decompose the latent actions into learnable motion and scene tokens to distinguish the robot's active movements from environmental changes, thus filtering out irrelevant dynamics. By distilling the learned latent actions into the latest VLA models, we achieve strong performance across both simulated (SIMPLER and LIBERO) and real-world robot settings. Notably, with only 10 real-world trajectories per task collected on a Franka robot, our approach successfully completes all five challenging tasks, demonstrating strong few-shot transferability in robotic manipulation.

keyInsights: Explicitly incorporating physical priors and action sequence prediction into latent action learning significantly bridges the gap between abstract visual changes and concrete executable robot actions.

Disentangling latent actions into distinct motion and scene components leads to cleaner, more structured representations, improving precision in motion understanding by filtering out irrelevant dynamics.
A novel knowledge distillation method can successfully transfer rich, physics-aware motion understanding to a generalist VLA model without compromising its inherent language reasoning capabilities.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models heavily rely on extensive datasets with explicit action labels, which are costly and time-consuming to acquire.

Prior latent action learning methods often neglect physical priors, lack task instruction guidance, use insufficient multi-frame inputs, and entangle robot motion with environmental changes, creating a semantic gap to executable actions.
Transferring learned latent actions to practical robot control, especially in few-shot scenarios, is hindered by these limitations, leading to poor generalization.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art success rates on the SIMPLER benchmark, with 78.0% in Visual Matching and 70.1% in Variant Aggregation for Google robots, and 87.5% for WidowX robots, marking improvements of 24.1% to 32.3% over baselines.
Attained the highest overall success rate of 98.0% across the LIBERO benchmark, including a 3.0% improvement over the π0.5 model on complex long-horizon tasks.

Demonstrated robust few-shot transferability in real-world Franka robot experiments, achieving up to 80% success with only 50 demonstrations for tasks where baselines entirely failed or performed substantially worse.
solution: LatBot proposes a decoupled latent action representation, separating robot-induced 'motion tokens' from 'scene tokens' that capture passive environmental changes, using a pre-trained VLM guided by specific tokens.

A unified latent action decoder, initialized from an image generation model, learns physically-grounded actions by jointly reconstructing future frames and generating inter-frame action sequences, with progressive fusion of scene and motion information.

A knowledge distillation strategy transfers the physically-grounded, disentangled latent action knowledge from the pre-trained Latent Action Model to a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) student model, using alignment and reasoning preservation losses, followed by action expert fine-tuning.
paper_summary.summary: LatBot introduces a framework for learning universal latent actions for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, distilling physically-grounded and disentangled action knowledge to enhance generalization and few-shot transferability. The approach achieves state-of-the-art results on manipulation benchmarks and demonstrates robust performance in real-world robotic tasks with limited demonstrations.

title: LatBot: Distilling Universal Latent Actions for Vision-Language-Action Models

abstract: Robotic manipulation requires understanding both the 3D spatial structure of the environment and its temporal evolution, yet most existing policies overlook one or both. They typically rely on 2D visual observations and backbones pretrained on static image--text pairs, resulting in high data requirements and limited understanding of environment dynamics. To address this, we introduce MV-VDP, a multi-view video diffusion policy that jointly models the 3D spatio-temporal state of the environment. The core idea is to simultaneously predict multi-view heatmap videos and RGB videos, which 1) align the representation format of video pretraining with action finetuning, and 2) specify not only what actions the robot should take, but also how the environment is expected to evolve in response to those actions. Extensive experiments show that MV-VDP enables data-efficient, robust, generalizable, and interpretable manipulation. With only ten demonstration trajectories and without additional pretraining, MV-VDP successfully performs complex real-world tasks, demonstrates strong robustness across a range of model hyperparameters, generalizes to out-of-distribution settings, and predicts realistic future videos. Experiments on Meta-World and real-world robotic platforms demonstrate that MV-VDP consistently outperforms video-prediction--based, 3D-based, and vision--language--action models, establishing a new state of the art in data-efficient multi-task manipulation.

keyInsights: Explicitly modeling both 3D spatial structure and temporal dynamics through a multi-view video diffusion framework significantly improves data efficiency and generalization in robotic manipulation.
Aligning the representation format of large-scale video pretraining with action fine-tuning via multi-view projections and heatmap representations helps bridge the perception-control gap.
Jointly predicting future visual observations alongside actions provides a mechanism for action validation and enhances interpretability and safety during robot deployment.
originalProblem: Existing robotic policies often rely on 2D visual observations, leading to a mismatch with 3D physical actions and limiting generalization under low-data regimes.
Many current approaches use backbones pretrained on static image-text pairs, failing to explicitly model temporal dynamics and anticipate environmental changes.
Contemporary Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often require large-scale datasets, hindering data efficiency for complex manipulation skills.
results: MV-VDP achieved an average success rate of 89.1% on seven Meta-World tasks with only five demonstrations per task, outperforming baselines like Diffusion Policy (37.7%).
On real-world basic manipulation tasks, it demonstrated a 57.1% average success rate with ten expert trajectories, surpassing DP3 (0%), "0.5 (1.4%), UVA (5.7%), and BridgeVLA (41.42%).

The model exhibited robustness to hyperparameter changes and achieved comparable performance with significantly fewer denoising steps (e.g., one step for ~89% success rate), enabling 5Hz inference speed, and reduced collision events to zero during human-validated deployments.
solution: The Multi-View Video Diffusion Policy (MV-VDP) leverages 3D-aware multi-view projections to implicitly encode 3D spatial structure from point clouds and robot end-effector poses.
A multi-view video diffusion transformer, built upon a 5B-parameter video foundation model (Wan2.2), jointly predicts future multi-view RGB videos and robot end-effector heatmap sequences.
Actions are decoded from the predicted multi-view heatmaps (for position) and denoised latent representations (for rotation and gripper state) to form continuous action chunks.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Beihang University, Tsinghua University, and collaborators developed the Multi-View Video Diffusion Policy (MV-VDP) for robotic manipulation, which integrates 3D structural priors and explicit temporal dynamics using a multi-view video diffusion model. This approach achieved an average success rate of 89.1% on Meta-World tasks with five demonstrations and 57.1% on real-world tasks with ten demonstrations, outperforming existing methods and enabling safer deployment through interpretable video predictions.

title: Multi-View Video Diffusion Policy: A 3D Spatio-Temporal-Aware Video Action Model

abstract: Predictive manipulation has recently gained considerable attention in the Embodied AI community due to its potential to improve robot policy performance by leveraging predicted states. However, generating accurate future visual states of robot-object interactions from world models remains a well-known challenge, particularly in achieving high-quality pixel-level representations. To this end, we propose LaDi-WM, a world model that predicts the latent space of future states using diffusion modeling. Specifically, LaDi-WM leverages the well-established latent space aligned with pre-trained Visual Foundation Models (VFMs), which comprises both geometric features (DINO-based) and semantic features (CLIP-based). We find that predicting the evolution of the latent space is easier to learn and more generalizable than directly predicting pixel-level images. Building on LaDi-WM, we design a diffusion policy that iteratively refines output actions by incorporating forecasted states, thereby generating more consistent and accurate results. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that LaDi-WM significantly enhances policy performance by 27.9\% on the LIBERO-LONG benchmark and 20\% on the real-world scenario. Furthermore, our world model and policies achieve impressive generalizability in real-world experiments.

organization_info.name: National University of Defense and Technology

keyInsights: Leveraging pre-trained Visual Foundation Models (DINOv2 for geometry, Siglip for semantics) creates a richer, manipulation-relevant latent space that is easier for a world model to learn and generalize.

An interactive diffusion mechanism, where geometric and semantic latent components influence each other via cross-attention, effectively models complex multimodal dynamics, leading to more aligned and accurate predictions.
Iterative refinement of robot actions based on progressively more accurate world model forecasts significantly improves policy precision and consistency at inference time.
originalProblem: Existing world models often struggle to generate accurate, high-quality future visual states, limiting their effectiveness for precise robot manipulation.

Latent spaces in current world models are typically optimized for image reconstruction and often fail to explicitly capture crucial geometric and semantic information essential for robotic manipulation.
Direct pixel-level prediction of future states is computationally expensive, prone to errors, and exhibits limited generalizability for dynamic robot-object interactions.
results: Achieved 68.7% average success rate on the LIBERO-LONG benchmark with 10 demonstrations, outperforming the previous state-of-the-art by 15.1%, and reached 90.7% with full data.
Demonstrated strong generalizability, improving real-world manipulation by 20% average success rate over vanilla Behavior Cloning and showing cross-scene transferability to the CALVIN D-D benchmark.

Ablation studies confirmed that the diffusion architecture, interactive latent space, and iterative refinement mechanism are crucial, with iterative refinement alone boosting performance by 19.9% in one iteration.
solution: LaDi-WM predicts future environmental dynamics by modeling them as a diffusion process in a generalizable latent space, derived by concatenating geometric features from DINOv2 and semantic features from Siglip.
A novel interactive diffusion process is proposed, utilizing cross-attention between separate denoising networks for geometric and semantic latent components to model their dynamic correlation.

An Imagination-Guided Diffusion Policy iteratively refines its action sequences by feeding initial actions to the trained LaDi-WM to generate imagined future states, which are then used to produce refined actions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the National University of Defense Technology, Peking University, and Shenzhen University developed LaDi-WM, a latent diffusion-based world model for predictive manipulation. This model leverages visual foundation models to construct a rich latent space and employs an iterative imagination-guided policy to achieve state-of-the-art performance on robotic tasks, outperforming previous methods by 15.1% in average success rate on the LIBERO-LONG benchmark with limited demonstrations.

title: LaDi-WM: A Latent Diffusion-based World Model for Predictive Manipulation

abstract: Training generalist policies for robotic manipulation has shown great promise, as they enable language-conditioned, multi-task behaviors across diverse scenarios. However, evaluating these policies remains difficult because real-world testing is expensive, time-consuming, and labor-intensive. It also requires frequent environment resets and carries safety risks when deploying unproven policies on physical robots. Manually creating and populating simulation environments with assets for robotic manipulation has not addressed these issues, primarily due to the significant engineering effort required and the substantial sim-to-real gap, both in terms of physics and rendering. In this paper, we explore the use of action-conditional video generation models as a scalable way to learn world models for policy evaluation. We demonstrate how to incorporate action conditioning into existing pre-trained video generation models. This allows leveraging internet-scale in-the-wild online videos during the pre-training stage and alleviates the need for a large dataset of paired video-action data, which is expensive to collect for robotic manipulation. Our paper examines the effect of dataset diversity, pre-trained weights, and common failure cases for the proposed evaluation pipeline. Our experiments demonstrate that across various metrics, including policy ranking and the correlation between actual policy values and predicted policy values, these models offer a promising approach for evaluating policies without requiring real-world interactions.
title: Scalable Policy Evaluation with Video World Models

abstract: Embodied world models have emerged as a promising paradigm in robotics, most of which leverage large-scale Internet videos or pretrained video generation models to enrich visual and motion priors. However, they still face key challenges: a misalignment between coordinate-space actions and pixel-space videos, sensitivity to camera viewpoint, and non-unified architectures across embodiments. To this end, we present BridgeV2W, which converts coordinate-space actions into pixel-aligned embodiment masks rendered from the URDF and camera parameters. These masks are then injected into a pretrained video generation model via a ControlNet-style pathway, which aligns the action control signals with predicted videos, adds view-specific conditioning to accommodate camera viewpoints, and yields a unified world model architecture across embodiments. To mitigate overfitting to static backgrounds, BridgeV2W further introduces a flow-based motion loss that focuses on learning dynamic and task-relevant regions. Experiments on single-arm (DROID) and dual-arm (AgiBot-G1) datasets, covering diverse and challenging conditions with unseen viewpoints and scenes, show that BridgeV2W improves video generation quality compared to prior state-of-the-art methods. We further demonstrate the potential of BridgeV2W on downstream real-world tasks, including policy evaluation and goal-conditioned planning. More results can be found on our project website at this https URL .

organization_info.name: New Laboratory of Pattern Recognition (NLPR), Institute of Automation, Chinese Academy of Sciences

keyInsights: Pixel-aligned embodiment masks effectively bridge the representational gap between abstract robot actions and pixel-space video generation, enabling more coherent and controllable predictions.
ControlNet-style conditioning allows pretrained video models to retain their rich visual and motion priors while receiving specific guidance from robot actions, preventing catastrophic forgetting.
The mask-based action representation naturally supports a unified world model architecture that can be applied across diverse robotic embodiments, from single-arm to dual-arm systems.
originalProblem: Existing embodied world models suffer from an "Action-Video Gap," where low-dimensional robot actions are misaligned with high-dimensional pixel-space video generation models.
Current EWMs often exhibit viewpoint sensitivity, struggling to generalize to unseen camera perspectives.
A lack of unified architectures for different robotic embodiments (e.g., single-arm vs. dual-arm) hinders scalability and knowledge transfer.
results: BridgeV2W consistently surpasses prior state-of-the-art methods in video generation quality (lower FVD and LPIPS) and action-to-video alignment (higher Mask-IoU) across single and dual-arm robot datasets.
The framework demonstrates robust generalization to unseen camera viewpoints and novel scenes, attributed to its pixel-aligned mask conditioning and leveraged visual priors.
BridgeV2W achieves a strong positive correlation (Pearson r = 0.84) with real-world policy success rates for policy evaluation and shows promising performance in goal-conditioned manipulation tasks.
solution: Robot actions are transformed into pixel-aligned 2D embodiment masks, either through URDF/camera parameters for calibrated robots or via segmentation tools like GroundedSAM for uncalibrated data.
A pretrained image-to-video diffusion model (e.g., CogVideoX-5B-I2V) is conditioned by these embodiment masks using a ControlNet-style pathway to guide video generation.
A novel flow-based motion loss is integrated into the training objective to prioritize dynamic, task-relevant regions over static backgrounds, improving temporal coherence and action alignment.
paper_summary.summary: BridgeV2W introduces a framework that integrates pretrained video generation models into embodied world models by converting robot actions into pixel-aligned embodiment masks. This approach enhances video quality, generalizes effectively to new viewpoints and scenes, and supports a unified architecture for various robotic systems.

title: BridgeV2W: Bridging Video Generation Models to Embodied World Models via Embodiment Masks

abstract: Real-world robotic manipulation demands visuomotor policies capable of robust spatial scene understanding and strong generalization across diverse camera viewpoints. While recent advances in 3D-aware visual representations have shown promise, they still suffer from several key limitations, including reliance on multi-view observations during inference which is impractical in single-view restricted scenarios, incomplete scene modeling that fails to capture holistic and fine-grained geometric structures essential for precise manipulation, and lack of effective policy training strategies to retain and exploit the acquired 3D knowledge. To address these challenges, we present MethodName, a unified representation-policy learning framework for view-generalizable robotic manipulation. MethodName introduces a single-view 3D pretraining paradigm that leverages point cloud reconstruction and feed-forward gaussian splatting under multi-view supervision to learn holistic geometric representations. During policy learning, MethodName performs multi-step distillation to preserve the pretrained geometric understanding and effectively transfer it to manipulation skills. We conduct experiments on 12 RLBench tasks, where our approach outperforms the previous state-of-the-art method by 12.7% in average success rate. Further evaluation on six representative tasks demonstrates strong zero-shot view generalization, with success rate drops of only 22.0% and 29.7% under moderate and large viewpoint shifts respectively, whereas the state-of-the-art method suffers larger decreases of 41.6% and 51.5%.

keyInsights: Robust, high-fidelity 3D scene representations, including both holistic geometry and fine-grained details, can be effectively learned from single-view inputs by combining advanced feature extraction and a coarse-to-fine reconstruction pipeline with Gaussian Splatting.

A multi-step latent distillation strategy provides an effective mechanism for transferring complex geometric understanding from a pretrained 3D encoder to a robotic manipulation policy, preventing representation drift and enhancing view generalization.

Utilizing Focal Loss during Gaussian Splatting pretraining improves the synthesis of novel views, yielding sharper textures and better consistency, which is crucial for dynamic and visually ambiguous regions.
originalProblem: Existing 3D-aware robotic manipulation methods often require multi-view observations for inference, which is impractical for real-world robotic deployment due to hardware and computational constraints.
Current single-view 3D perception techniques frequently produce coarse scene models, failing to capture the fine-grained geometric details necessary for precise manipulation tasks.

Traditional policy training strategies, such as pretrain-finetune or joint-training, struggle to effectively transfer acquired 3D knowledge, leading to representation drift, overfitting to training viewpoints, and poor generalization.
results: GEM3D achieves an average success rate of 44.2% across 12 RLBench tasks, representing a 12.7% absolute improvement over the previous state-of-the-art, ManiGaussian.

The framework demonstrates superior zero-shot view generalization, with only a 22.03% success rate drop under moderate viewpoint shifts compared to 41.62% for ManiGaussian, and up to 6x higher success rates than PerAct under large shifts.

Qualitatively and quantitatively (PSNR +7.08 dB, SSIM +0.4298, Chamfer L2 -0.0289), GEM3D's pretraining yields significantly sharper 3D reconstructions and more consistent novel views than ManiGaussian, confirming its enhanced spatial understanding.
solution: A Geometrically-Grounded Pretraining phase learns holistic and fine-grained 3D scene representations from single RGB-D inputs, integrating coarse-to-fine point cloud reconstruction with feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting.

A Distillation-based Policy Learning phase transfers the learned 3D knowledge to a visuomotor policy using multi-step latent distillation, aligning the policy encoder's tokens with a frozen, pretrained 3D feature extractor.
The pretraining incorporates Deformable Cross-Attention for detailed geometric recovery and uses Focal Loss for supervising Gaussian rendering to improve robustness to dynamic appearance changes.
paper_summary.summary: GEM3D introduces a framework for view-generalizable robotic manipulation, utilizing geometrically-grounded 3D visual representations derived from single-view observations. The system achieves a 44.2% average success rate across 12 RLBench tasks, surpassing previous state-of-the-art by 12.7% and exhibiting enhanced robustness to diverse camera viewpoints.

title: Learning Geometrically-Grounded 3D Visual Representations for View-Generalizable Robotic Manipulation

abstract: This paper presents the World-Action Model (WAM), an action-regularized world model that jointly reasons over future visual observations and the actions that drive state transitions. Unlike conventional world models trained solely via image prediction, WAM incorporates an inverse dynamics objective into DreamerV2 that predicts actions from latent state transitions, encouraging the learned representations to capture action-relevant structure critical for downstream control. We evaluate WAM on enhancing policy learning across eight manipulation tasks from the CALVIN benchmark. We first pretrain a diffusion policy via behavioral cloning on world model latents, then refine it with model-based PPO inside the frozen world model. Without modifying the policy architecture or training procedure, WAM improves average behavioral cloning success from 59.4% to 71.2% over DreamerV2 and DiWA baselines. After PPO fine-tuning, WAM achieves 92.8% average success versus 79.8% for the baseline, with two tasks reaching 100%, using 8.7x fewer training steps.

keyInsights: Explicitly regularizing world model latent representations through an inverse dynamics objective compels the encoder to capture more precise and causally relevant information about environment dynamics.

Operating the inverse dynamics head on encoder embeddings, rather than higher-level RSSM features, ensures that action-aware structure is cascaded through the recurrent model, benefiting both posterior and prior states for imagined rollouts.

This lightweight, complementary augmentation to existing world model backbones significantly enhances the quality of learned representations and improves downstream policy learning without a complete architectural overhaul.
originalProblem: Conventional world models primarily optimize for future observation prediction, leading to latent representations that may lack explicit action-relevant structure critical for effective robotic control.
The asymmetry in training objectives means learned latent states often overlook fine-grained details about how actions influence the environment, impeding downstream policy learning.
Existing solutions like unified action-world models often require significant architectural redesigns or reliance on large foundation models, limiting their practical applicability.
results: WAM achieved superior imagination quality compared to DreamerV2, demonstrating better PSNR (22.10 vs 21.66), SSIM (0.814 vs 0.807), LPIPS (0.144 vs 0.149), and FVD (10.82 vs 12.13) with 8.7 times fewer world model training steps.

Policies trained with WAM's representations on the CALVIN benchmark showed improved average behavioral cloning success (61.7% vs 45.8% for DiWA) and higher average success after PPO fine-tuning (92.8% vs 79.8% for DiWA).

WAM demonstrated notable performance gains in tasks requiring precise control, such as closing a drawer (+31.1 percentage points in BC) and opening a drawer (+22.26 percentage points in fine-tuning) compared to the DiWA baseline.
solution: The World-Action Model (WAM) augments the DreamerV2 world model architecture by incorporating an inverse dynamics head.
This head predicts the action that caused a transition between two consecutive encoder embeddings, explicitly regularizing the latent representations to capture action-relevant information.

After training, WAM's action-regularized latent space is used as a frozen simulator for efficient policy optimization, enabling both behavioral cloning and model-based reinforcement learning fine-tuning.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at The Ohio State University developed the World-Action Model (WAM), an extension of world models that uses an inverse dynamics objective to regularize latent representations for action-relevant information. WAM achieved improved imagination quality with 8.7 times fewer training steps than DreamerV2, and policies trained with WAM demonstrated significantly higher success rates on robotic manipulation tasks, including a 15.9 percentage point average gain in behavioral cloning and a 13.0 percentage point average gain after model-based fine-tuning on the CALVIN benchmark.

title: Enhancing Policy Learning with World-Action Model

abstract: In this work, we present an approach to construct a video-based robot policy capable of reliably executing diverse tasks across different robots and environments from few video demonstrations without using any action annotations. Our method leverages images as a task-agnostic representation, encoding both the state and action information, and text as a general representation for specifying robot goals. By synthesizing videos that ``hallucinate'' robot executing actions and in combination with dense correspondences between frames, our approach can infer the closed-formed action to execute to an environment without the need of any explicit action labels. This unique capability allows us to train the policy solely based on RGB videos and deploy learned policies to various robotic tasks. We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach in learning policies on table-top manipulation and navigation tasks. Additionally, we contribute an open-source framework for efficient video modeling, enabling the training of high-fidelity policy models with four GPUs within a single day.

keyInsights: Inferring 3D actions from 2D optical flow between synthesized video frames and initial depth allows for policy learning and execution without requiring explicit action labels.
Factorized spatial-temporal convolutions in diffusion models enable training high-fidelity video generation models on 4 GPUs in a single day, dramatically improving computational accessibility.
A replanning strategy that re-synthesizes video plans based on current observations is critical for achieving robust, closed-loop control and mitigating accumulated errors in long-horizon tasks.
originalProblem: Robot learning methods traditionally rely on expensive, robot-specific action labels, even for video-based approaches, hindering scalability and data efficiency.
Current robot policies struggle with generalizability across diverse tasks, robot embodiments, and environments due to varying state and action representations.
State-of-the-art video generation models for robot learning demand immense computational resources, creating a high barrier to entry for research and adoption.
results: Achieved an average success rate of 43.1% across 11 Meta-World manipulation tasks, outperforming behavioral cloning baselines (15.4-16.2%) and prior methods like UniPi (6.1%).
Demonstrated successful zero-shot cross-embodiment transfer, with a model trained on human videos achieving 90% success controlling a simulated robot in a pushing task.
Successfully deployed on a real-world Franka Emika Panda robot for tabletop manipulation, generalizing from a large dataset to a new setup with minimal fine-tuning.
solution: A three-stage pipeline (video generation, flow prediction, action regression) is introduced to infer robot actions directly from synthesized future video plans without explicit action labels.

An efficient conditional diffusion model, utilizing factorized spatial-temporal convolutions, generates high-fidelity future video frames from initial RGBD observations and text prompts on commodity hardware.
3D rigid body transformations for objects or the robot are regressed by lifting 2D optical flow from synthesized videos and initial depth, incorporating a replanning strategy for closed-loop control.
paper_summary.summary: The Actions from Video Dense Correspondences (AVDC) framework enables robots to learn and execute tasks solely from actionless video demonstrations by synthesizing future video plans using a conditional diffusion model and inferring 3D robot actions from the dense optical flow between synthesized frames and initial depth information. This approach achieves 43.1% average success across 11 Meta-World manipulation tasks and demonstrates successful zero-shot transfer from human videos to robot control, all while significantly reducing computational training costs.

title: Learning to Act from Actionless Videos through Dense Correspondences

abstract: Latent action representations learned from unlabeled videos have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for pretraining vision-language-action (VLA) models without explicit robot action supervision. However, latent actions derived solely from RGB observations primarily encode appearance-driven dynamics and lack explicit 3D geometric structure, which is essential for precise and contact-rich manipulation. To address this limitation, we introduce UniLACT, a transformer-based VLA model that incorporates geometric structure through depth-aware latent pretraining, enabling downstream policies to inherit stronger spatial priors. To facilitate this process, we propose UniLARN, a unified latent action learning framework based on inverse and forward dynamics objectives that learns a shared embedding space for RGB and depth while explicitly modeling their cross-modal interactions. This formulation produces modality-specific and unified latent action representations that serve as pseudo-labels for the depth-aware pretraining of UniLACT. Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world settings demonstrate the effectiveness of depth-aware unified latent action representations. UniLACT consistently outperforms RGB-based latent action baselines under in-domain and out-of-domain pretraining regimes, as well as on both seen and unseen manipulation tasks.

organization_info.name: University of North Carolina at Charlotte

keyInsights: Embedding 3D geometric structure directly into the latent action space significantly enriches spatial priors for VLA models, crucial for precise manipulation.
Unified latent actions learned through cross-modal inverse and forward dynamics models effectively integrate complementary dynamics from both RGB and depth modalities.
Unsupervised pretraining with depth-aware latent actions from diverse data leads to enhanced generalization and robust performance, particularly in geometry-centric tasks.
originalProblem: Existing latent action learning methods for VLA models primarily use RGB observations, lacking explicit 3D geometric understanding.
This absence of spatial reasoning hinders performance in contact-rich robot manipulation tasks requiring precise grasping, placement, and collision avoidance.
Prior approaches incorporating depth often require substantial robot-annotated trajectory data, limiting scalability and data efficiency.
results: In simulation, UNILACT achieved a +29.2% relative improvement in average sequence length over RGB-only baselines when pretrained on out-of-domain data, demonstrating stronger generalization.
Real-world experiments showed UNILACT achieving a 10% overall improvement in average success rate (62.5%) compared to RGB-only baselines (52.5%), with up to +20% gains on geometry-centric tasks.
The framework maintains computational efficiency, exhibiting the same model size (89.8M parameters) and per-step inference latency (27ms) as RGB-only baselines during deployment.
solution: A framework named UNILARN learns modality-specific (RGB and depth) and unified latent action representations from unlabeled RGB-D video observations using a two-stage vector-quantization pipeline.
UNILACT, a transformer-based VLA model, is then pretrained with these depth-aware unified latent actions to internalize complementary semantic and geometric cues.

The model is fine-tuned to generate continuous robot control commands, critically operating solely on RGB observations and task instructions during inference without requiring depth sensors at deployment.
paper_summary.summary: UNILACT introduces a framework for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models that integrates 3D geometric understanding directly into latent action representations during unsupervised pretraining, addressing the spatial reasoning limitations of RGB-only approaches. This method improved real-world robot manipulation success rates by 10% and exhibited superior generalization in simulations without increasing inference time computational overhead.

title: UniLACT: Depth-Aware RGB Latent Action Learning for Vision-Language-Action Models

abstract: We present a method to generate video-action pairs that follow text instructions, starting from an initial image observation and the robot's joint states. Our approach automatically provides action labels for video diffusion models, overcoming the common lack of action annotations and enabling their full use for robotic policy learning. Existing methods either adopt two-stage pipelines, which limit tightly coupled cross-modal information sharing, or rely on adapting a single-modal diffusion model for a joint distribution that cannot fully leverage pretrained video knowledge. To overcome these limitations, we (1) extend a pretrained video diffusion model with a parallel, dedicated action diffusion model that preserves pretrained knowledge, (2) introduce a Bridge Attention mechanism to enable effective cross-modal interaction, and (3) design an action refinement module to convert coarse actions into precise controls for low-resolution datasets. Extensive evaluations on multiple public benchmarks and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method generates higher-quality videos, more accurate actions, and significantly outperforms existing baselines, offering a scalable framework for leveraging large-scale video data for robotic learning.

keyInsights: Leveraging the general visual knowledge of a powerful pretrained video diffusion model in parallel with a dedicated action diffusion model is more effective than adapting it to a joint latent space from scratch.

The proposed Bridge Attention mechanism is crucial for enabling effective cross-modal interaction between video and action modalities without compromising their distinct representations or leading to information distortion.

An action refinement module can successfully convert coarse actions generated from lower-resolution video inputs into precise control signals, which is vital for achieving high success rates in fine-grained robotic manipulation tasks.
originalProblem: The scarcity of direct, paired action labels for large-scale video datasets limits the application of powerful video diffusion models in robotic policy learning.
Two-stage methods for robotic control suffer from heavy dependence on generated video quality and a lack of tight coupling between visual prediction and action inference.
Existing joint video-action models often require extensive data to train from scratch or adapt pretrained models to a shared latent space, leading to suboptimal performance with limited expert data.
results: CoVAR consistently outperformed all joint-model baselines in video generation quality across multiple metrics (PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS, FVD) on Calvin and Libero90 datasets, producing clearer videos with fewer artifacts.

Achieved substantially higher action success rates on the Calvin dataset (e.g., 1.000 for 'Drawer' tasks) and demonstrated robust performance on a real-world UR5 dataset for fine-grained manipulation (e.g., 0.74 for Screw pick).

Ablation studies confirmed that the Bridge Attention mechanism and the UNet-based action decoder are critical for performance, with their removal leading to significant degradations in both video quality and action success rates.
solution: Introduces a multi-modal diffusion architecture that extends a pretrained video diffusion model (OpenSora-1.2) with a parallel, dedicated Action Diffusion Transformer, both conditioned on text and initial robot states.
Employs a Multi-Modal Rectified Flow to learn a simplified, straight-line path from noise to data, accelerating training convergence and enhancing the quality of generated video-action pairs.
Incorporates a Bridge Attention mechanism that uses separate queries, keys, and values for video and action features to enable effective, low-interference cross-modal information exchange.
paper_summary.summary: CoVAR develops a multi-modal diffusion framework that co-generates videos and precise robotic actions conditioned on initial observations and language instructions. The system achieved higher action success rates and superior video quality compared to existing methods on various benchmarks, demonstrating improved precision for robotic manipulation tasks.

title: CoVAR: Co-generation of Video and Action for Robotic Manipulation via Multi-Modal Diffusion

abstract: Learned world models hold significant potential for robotic manipulation, as they can serve as simulator for real-world interactions. While extensive progress has been made in 2D video-based world models, these approaches often lack geometric and spatial reasoning, which is essential for capturing the physical structure of the 3D world. To address this limitation, we introduce iMoWM, a novel interactive world model designed to generate color images, depth maps, and robot arm masks in an autoregressive manner conditioned on actions. To overcome the high computational cost associated with three-dimensional information, we propose MMTokenizer, which unifies multi-modal inputs into a compact token representation. This design enables iMoWM to leverage large-scale pretrained VideoGPT models while maintaining high efficiency and incorporating richer physical information. With its multi-modal representation, iMoWM not only improves the visual quality of future predictions but also serves as an effective simulator for model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) and facilitates real-world imitation learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of iMoWM across these tasks, showcasing the advantages of multi-modal world modeling for robotic manipulation. Homepage: this https URL

keyInsights: Explicitly integrating geometric information (depth maps) and dynamic attention (robot arm masks) alongside color images into world models significantly improves prediction accuracy and physical consistency for robotic interaction.

Developing an efficient multi-modal tokenization strategy allows for the effective incorporation of rich 3D information into transformer-based world models without incurring prohibitive computational costs.

Action-conditioned multi-modal world models can serve as high-fidelity neural simulators, enabling faster policy learning in reinforcement learning and providing valuable data augmentation for imitation learning.
originalProblem: Existing 2D video-based world models lack explicit geometric and spatial understanding, which is crucial for effective robotic manipulation.

Direct 4D world models, such as those using 3D Gaussian Splatting, are computationally expensive and require high-quality 3D reconstruction, making them impractical for common monocular camera setups.
Prior models struggled to efficiently provide comprehensive, interactive multi-modal predictions (color, depth, and robot dynamics) necessary for robust downstream robotic tasks.
results: iMoWM outperforms state-of-the-art baselines like iVideoGPT and GWM in action-conditioned multi-modal video generation, achieving superior FVD, PSNR, SSIM, and notably, depth accuracy (AbsRel) on BAIR and RoboNet datasets.

The model demonstrates faster convergence and higher ultimate success rates in visual model-based reinforcement learning across Meta-World tasks compared to prior image-only and 3DGS-based approaches.

High-fidelity multi-modal roll-outs generated by iMoWM effectively augment real-world demonstration data, enabling imitation learning policies to achieve success rates comparable to those trained solely on ground-truth data for real-robot manipulation tasks.
solution: A novel MMTokenizer unifies multi-modal inputs (RGB, depth, robot arm masks) into a compact, discrete token representation, optimizing for dynamic regions and incorporating modality-specific embeddings.
An autoregressive transformer, initialized from pre-trained checkpoints, predicts future multi-modal tokens by integrating action information via 'slot tokens' representing robot end-effector poses.

The world model is applied as an imagined simulator for model-based reinforcement learning and as a data augmentation tool for real-world imitation learning, generating synthetic multi-modal trajectories.
paper_summary.summary: iMoWM presents an interactive multi-modal world model that predicts future color images, depth maps, and robot arm masks conditioned on actions. This model leverages an efficient multi-modal tokenizer and an autoregressive transformer to achieve enhanced visual prediction, faster learning in model-based reinforcement learning, and high-fidelity data augmentation for real-world imitation learning.

title: iMoWM: Taming Interactive Multi-Modal World Model for Robotic Manipulation

abstract: Modeling generalized robot control policies poses ongoing challenges for language-guided robot manipulation tasks. Existing methods often struggle to efficiently utilize cross-dataset resources or rely on resource-intensive vision-language models, thus limiting their multi-task performance and practical applications. In this study, we propose a novel approach that decouples robot action trajectory encoding and control policy generation by leveraging latent action trajectory spaces, enhancing the generalization ability of policy generation on multi-task manipulation tasks. First, we pre-train a task-agnostic auto-encoder to project an action trajectory of several frames accompanied with observations into a latent action trajectory space on large-scale datasets collected with multiple embodiments in various environments. Then we propose learning a diffusion model based on the latent action trajectory space to generate actions of next steps. Through experiments on two widely used benchmarks, results demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms baselines by 7%-29% in terms of average success rate across eight tasks. Our method can consistently benefit from pre-training while baselines cannot. Our method is more than two times faster than our baseline.
title: RoLD: Robot Latent Diffusion for Multi-task Policy Modeling

abstract: World-model-based imagine-then-act becomes a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation, yet existing approaches typically support either purely image-based forecasting or reasoning over partial 3D geometry, limiting their ability to predict complete 4D scene dynamics. This work proposes a novel embodied 4D world model that enables geometrically consistent, arbitrary-view RGBD generation: given only a single-view RGBD observation as input, the model imagines the remaining viewpoints, which can then be back-projected and fused to assemble a more complete 3D structure across time. To efficiently learn the multi-view, cross-modality generation, we explicitly design cross-view and cross-modality feature fusion that jointly encourage consistency between RGB and depth and enforce geometric alignment across views. Beyond prediction, converting generated futures into actions is often handled by inverse dynamics, which is ill-posed because multiple actions can explain the same transition. We address this with a test-time action optimization strategy that backpropagates through the generative model to infer a trajectory-level latent best matching the predicted future, and a residual inverse dynamics model that turns this trajectory prior into accurate executable actions. Experiments on three datasets demonstrate strong performance on both 4D scene generation and downstream manipulation, and ablations provide practical insights into the key design choices.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Explicit architectural designs that promote cross-modality fusion and geometry-aware cross-view attention are essential for generating coherent and physically plausible 4D dynamic scene predictions.
Representing an entire action sequence as a compact, low-dimensional latent code enables a more stable and robust conditioning for generative models, capturing the temporal rhythm of manipulation.
Combining test-time optimization of trajectory latents with a residual inverse dynamics module effectively mitigates the ill-posedness of action inference, leading to more reliable robot control.
originalProblem: Robotic world models often provide 2D image predictions lacking geometric consistency or partial 3D geometry that is incomplete and brittle under occlusion, which is insufficient for precise physical interaction.
Ensuring coherence between generated RGB and depth information, and maintaining geometric consistency across multiple camera views for 4D dynamic scene generation, remains a significant challenge.

Inferring robust robot actions from predicted future states is an ill-posed problem, as traditional inverse dynamics models struggle with ambiguity and often ignore the temporal structure of manipulation trajectories.
results: The model consistently outperforms baselines in 4D scene generation, showing lower depth errors (AbRel, RMSE) and superior 3D point cloud quality (Chamfer Distance, EMD) on both synthetic and real-world datasets.
MVISTA-4D achieves higher manipulation success rates across various tasks, reaching 72.6% on RLBench and demonstrating superior performance on a real robot compared to existing approaches.

Ablation studies confirm that the proposed geometry-aware deformable cross-view attention, local cross-modality attention, and trajectory-level action inference mechanisms are critical for enhancing both 4D prediction fidelity and robotic manipulation success.
solution: MVISTA-4D introduces a latent video diffusion model enhanced with structured tokenization, learnable modality tokens, and local cross-modality attention for consistent RGB-D output.
A novel spherical camera embedding and geometry-aware deformable cross-view attention mechanism enforce explicit geometric consistency across arbitrary queried viewpoints in the generated 4D scenes.
Action inference is achieved through test-time optimization of a low-dimensional latent code representing the entire action trajectory, further refined by a residual inverse dynamics module.
paper_summary.summary: MVISTA-4D develops a view-consistent 4D world model for robotic manipulation, generating multi-view RGB-D predictions of dynamic scenes and inferring actions. The system achieves improved manipulation success rates and more accurate 4D scene understanding by integrating explicit cross-modality and cross-view consistency with a trajectory-level action inference strategy.

title: MVISTA-4D: View-Consistent 4D World Model with Test-Time Action Inference for Robotic Manipulation

abstract: Visual imitation learning enables robotic agents to acquire skills by observing expert demonstration videos. In the one-shot setting, the agent generates a policy after observing a single expert demonstration without additional fine-tuning.
